proposals.title: Tunable-Conductivity Green-Synthesized Ni6MnO8/Graphene Oxide Nanocomposites for Optimized Photocatalytic Degradation and Electrochemical Sensing
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in photocatalytic and electrochemical materials often prioritizes maximizing electrical conductivity. However, for applications like photocatalysis and selective electrochemical sensing, moderate or tunable conductivity can be more critical, as it influences charge carrier separation, recombination rates, and interfacial interactions. Developing green-synthesized nanoparticles with tailored conductivity profiles offers a sustainable pathway to optimize performance for specific environmental and energy applications beyond simply achieving the highest conductivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by controlling the reduction level and interfacial bonding of graphene oxide (GO) within green-synthesized Ni6MnO8 nanocomposites, we can precisely tune the composite's electrical conductivity, and that an intermediate, optimized conductivity will yield superior photocatalytic degradation kinetics and electrochemical sensing selectivity compared to either highly conductive or insulating analogues.
proposals.method: We will employ a green, amine-assisted hydrothermal synthesis (inspired by Salavati-Niasari's prior work) to produce Ni6MnO8 nanostructures. These will be composited with graphene oxide (GO) via sonication, followed by controlled thermal or chemical reduction to create a series of nanocomposites with graded GO reduction levels (from pristine GO to reduced GO). Characterization will involve XRD, FESEM, TEM, XPS, and four-point probe conductivity measurements. Photocatalytic activity will be evaluated against methylene blue and rhodamine B under visible light, while electrochemical properties will be tested for hydrogen peroxide and dopamine sensing using cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a framework for valuing tunable conductivity over maximum conductivity in nanocomposite design. It will provide a clear correlation between controlled GO reduction, composite conductivity, and application-specific performance (photocatalysis vs. sensing), contributing new, sustainable material candidates for water treatment and biosensing. The findings will challenge the prevailing 'higher conductivity is always better' paradigm in functional nanomaterials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in intentionally de-optimizing for maximum conductivity to find a performance 'sweet spot' for specific applications, using a green synthesis route for the core perovskite material. This approach integrates Salavati-Niasari's expertise in photocatalytic nanostructures and perovskite synthesis with graphene composites for a fundamentally different design goal.

proposals.title: Correlating Green Synthesis Pathways with Single-Particle Conductivity in Graphene-Enhanced Perovskite Nanocatalysts for Photocatalysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Salavati-Niasari and others demonstrates that green synthesis parameters profoundly influence the morphology and photocatalytic activity of nanostructured oxides and perovskites. However, the fundamental electrical properties of individual nanoparticles, which govern charge carrier dynamics in photocatalysis and electrochemical storage, remain obscured by ensemble-averaging techniques. Establishing a direct link between synthesis conditions, nanoscale conductivity, and macroscopic performance is a critical, unresolved challenge in designing efficient nanomaterials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the amine-assisted green synthesis parameters for materials like Ni6MnO8 or Lu2CrMnO6 directly control the crystallinity and defect density at the single-particle level, leading to measurable variations in electrical conductivity that correlate with their photocatalytic and electrochemical hydrogen storage efficiency, especially when composited with conductive graphene derivatives.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of nanostructures (e.g., Ni6MnO8, Lu2CrMnO6, and their graphene nanocomposites) using established amine-mediated hydrothermal/solvothermal methods, varying the amine type (ethylenediamine, triethylamine) as a key morphological parameter. Individual nanoparticles will be dispersed on an insulating SiO2/Si substrate with pre-fabricated gold micro-electrodes. Single-particle electrical conductivity will be measured using Conductive Atomic Force Microscopy (C-AFM) and Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM), employing point-contact current-voltage (I-V) spectroscopy. Data will be analyzed using the Landauer-Büttiker formalism for ballistic transport and standard models for ohmic and Schottky contacts to extract specific conductivity values.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, single-particle conductivity map for green-synthesized perovskite and spinel nanomaterials, directly linking synthetic chemistry to a fundamental electronic property. It will establish a new structure-property-performance paradigm, moving beyond ensemble measurements to guide the rational design of nanocatalysts and electrode materials with optimized charge transport for environmental and energy applications.
proposals.novelty: While ensemble properties are well-studied, applying single-particle scanning probe conductivity measurements to correlate green synthesis pathways (amine selection) with the electronic properties of complex perovskite and spinel nanostructures is a novel and unexplored approach, offering unprecedented insight into intrinsic material behavior.

proposals.title: Graphene-Supported Nickel Manganese Oxide Nanohybrids for the Adsorptive Recovery and Photocatalytic Stabilization of Polyphenols from Agricultural Wastewater
proposals.motivation: Agricultural and food processing waste streams contain valuable bioactive phytochemicals like polyphenols, but current recovery methods are often inefficient and non-selective. Concurrently, pre-2026 research highlights the need for advanced nanomaterials that combine high adsorption capacity with protective functions to prevent the degradation of these sensitive compounds post-extraction. Developing a sustainable, integrated capture and stabilization platform addresses both resource recovery and environmental remediation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that amine-modified Ni-Mn oxide nanostructures, integrated with reduced graphene oxide (rGO), will exhibit synergistic high adsorption affinity for specific polyphenols and photocatalytic activity under visible light to degrade co-existing organic pollutants, thereby creating a cleaner stream for phytochemical recovery and stabilizing the captured compounds.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Ni6MnO8 nanostructures using amine-assisted hydrothermal methods (inspired by Salavati-Niasari's prior work) to control morphology and surface chemistry. These will be composited with rGO via in-situ reduction to enhance surface area and electron transfer. The nanohybrids will be tested as substrates in batch adsorption experiments using model polyphenol solutions and real pomegranate peel wastewater. Adsorption kinetics and isotherms will be modeled. Concurrent photocatalytic degradation of dye pollutants (like methylene blue) in the mixture will be evaluated under visible light to assess the material's dual function.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, multifunctional nanomaterial designed specifically for the valorization of agricultural waste. It will provide a mechanistic understanding of the adsorption-photocatalysis synergy for simultaneous phytochemical recovery and water purification, advancing beyond single-function materials. The project will expand the application of double perovskite and transition metal oxide nanostructures into sustainable chemistry and green technology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate design of a nanomaterial (Ni6MnO8/rGO) not just for degradation, but primarily for the targeted capture and preservation of valuable molecules from complex waste streams, integrating Salavati-Niasari's expertise in photocatalytic nanostructures with a new application in selective adsorption and resource recovery.

proposals.title: Non-Thermal Synthesis of Co/Co3O4-Graphene Nanocomposites via Sol-Gel and Hydrothermal Routes for Enhanced Photocatalytic Degradation of Organic Pollutants
proposals.motivation: Co/Co3O4 heterostructures are promising photocatalysts due to their synergistic redox activity and visible-light absorption. However, conventional thermal decomposition synthesis often leads to particle aggregation and poor interfacial contact with conductive supports like graphene, limiting charge transfer efficiency. Developing mild, solution-based synthesis routes is crucial to achieve fine-tuned nanostructures for superior photocatalytic performance in water remediation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that employing non-thermal sol-gel and hydrothermal methods will yield well-dispersed Co/Co3O4 nanoparticles with intimate integration onto graphene sheets. This tailored architecture will facilitate efficient charge separation and enhanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of organic dyes under visible light.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Co/Co3O4 nanoparticles via a citric acid-assisted sol-gel method using cobalt nitrate, followed by controlled calcination at low temperatures (250-400°C). In parallel, a hydrothermal route will be employed using cobalt precursors and a reducing agent (e.g., NaBH4) in an autoclave. Both sets of nanoparticles will be composited with graphene oxide (GO) through in-situ or ex-situ mixing, followed by reduction to form reduced graphene oxide (rGO) composites. The materials will be characterized using XRD, FESEM, TEM, XPS, UV-Vis DRS, and PL spectroscopy. Photocatalytic activity will be evaluated by monitoring the degradation of methylene blue and rhodamine B under visible-light irradiation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a comparative framework for non-thermal synthesis routes in creating efficient Co/Co3O4-based photocatalysts. It will provide fundamental insights into how synthesis-driven morphology and interface engineering with graphene influence charge carrier dynamics. The resulting optimized nanocomposite is expected to demonstrate significantly higher degradation rates and stability than counterparts prepared by thermal decomposition.
proposals.novelty: While Co3O4-graphene composites exist, the deliberate synthesis of mixed-valence Co/Co3O4 heterojunctions via these specific low-energy pathways, with a direct comparison of sol-gel versus hydrothermal effects on the photocatalytic mechanism, is novel. This approach moves beyond thermal decomposition to achieve superior interfacial control.

proposals.title: Systematic Investigation of Long-Term Oxidation Resistance and Magnetic Property Retention in Graphene-Encapsulated Transition Metal Oxide Nanostructures for Robust Photocatalytic Applications
proposals.motivation: The long-term stability of functional nanoparticles, particularly their resistance to oxidation and retention of magnetic properties, is a critical but often overlooked factor limiting their practical deployment in environmental remediation and energy storage. Pre-2026 research by groups like Salavati-Niasari's has excelled in synthesizing high-performance photocatalytic and hydrogen storage nanomaterials, but systematic studies on their aging under operational conditions are scarce. Understanding degradation pathways is essential for designing durable nanocatalysts and electrodes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that encapsulating synthesized transition metal oxide nanostructures (e.g., Ni6MnO8, Co2SiO4, Lu2CrMnO6) within conductive, impermeable graphene shells will significantly enhance their long-term oxidation resistance and preserve their intrinsic magnetic and photocatalytic properties compared to their bare counterparts.
proposals.method: We will synthesize core-shell nanostructures via a two-step process: first, preparing the core nanoparticles (Ni6MnO8, Co2SiO4) using established amine-assisted sol-gel or hydrothermal methods from the researcher's portfolio; second, encapsulating them with few-layer graphene via chemical vapor deposition (CVD) using a methane precursor. Accelerated aging tests will be conducted by exposing samples to controlled humid air and elevated temperatures. Long-term stability will be characterized by monitoring oxidation via X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) at intervals over 6 months. Magnetic property retention will be quantified using vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM), and photocatalytic activity decay will be tracked via dye degradation tests under visible light.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a clear structure-property relationship between graphene encapsulation and the long-term functional stability of complex oxide nanomaterials. It will provide critical durability data, moving beyond initial activity reports to enable the design of robust, commercially viable nanocatalysts. The findings will directly inform the development of stable electrodes for hydrogen storage and photocatalytic systems for wastewater treatment.
proposals.novelty: While graphene coating is known for protection, its systematic application and efficacy evaluation for preserving the magnetic and photocatalytic functions of the researcher's specific, novel multi-metal oxide nanostructures over extended periods is unexplored. This bridges the gap between innovative synthesis and practical, long-term application.

proposals.title: Graphene-Quantum Dot Sensitized Co@Co3O4 Core-Shell Nanostructures for Enhanced Visible-Light Photocatalytic Degradation of Persistent Organic Pollutants
proposals.motivation: The efficient photocatalytic degradation of persistent organic pollutants (POPs) in water remains a critical challenge. While Co3O4 is a promising p-type semiconductor photocatalyst, its wide bandgap and rapid charge recombination limit its visible-light activity. Core-shell structures, particularly those combining a metallic core with an oxide shell, offer a promising route to enhance charge separation and light absorption via plasmonic and heterojunction effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthesizing Co@Co3O4 core-shell nanoparticles and integrating them with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) will create a Z-scheme heterojunction. This architecture will significantly improve visible-light harvesting, suppress electron-hole recombination, and boost photocatalytic degradation efficiency compared to individual components.
proposals.method: We will synthesize metallic Co nanoparticles via a thermal decomposition method using oleylamine as both solvent and capping agent, based on Salavati-Niasari's expertise with amine-mediated morphologies. A controlled, partial oxidation process in air will form the Co3O4 shell, creating the core-shell structure. These nanostructures will then be composited with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), synthesized from citric acid and urea, via a sonication-assisted assembly. The photocatalytic performance will be evaluated for degrading methylene blue and tetracycline under visible light, with detailed characterization using XRD, TEM, XPS, UV-Vis DRS, and PL spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel synthesis strategy for tunable Co/Co3O4 core-shell systems and demonstrate a new, efficient Z-scheme photocatalytic platform combining plasmonic cores, p-n heterojunctions, and GQD sensitizers. It will contribute fundamental insights into charge transfer mechanisms in such complex architectures and advance the design of non-precious metal-based photocatalysts for environmental remediation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate construction of a triple-component photocatalytic system where the metallic Co core acts as a plasmonic sensitizer and charge mediator, the Co3O4 shell forms a heterojunction, and the GQDs serve as both photosensitizers and electron reservoirs, a configuration not extensively explored pre-2026 for Co-based systems.

proposals.title: Enhancing Photocatalytic Hydrogen Evolution via In-Situ Growth of Cobalt Silicate (CSO) on Exfoliated g-C3N4 Nanosheets: A Study on Precursor Chemistry and Loading Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a promising visible-light photocatalyst, but its efficiency is limited by rapid charge recombination. Coupling it with cobalt silicate (CSO), a known co-catalyst for hydrogen evolution, can mitigate this, but conventional composite preparation methods (e.g., physical mixing, simple impregnation) often yield poor CSO dispersion and weak interfacial contact, hindering charge transfer. Optimizing the synthesis to achieve uniform, high-loading CSO on g-C3N4 is therefore critical for advancing practical solar fuel generation systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that employing a controlled in-situ solvothermal synthesis, using specific cobalt and silicon precursors in the presence of pre-exfoliated g-C3N4 nanosheets, will lead to the nucleation and growth of well-dispersed, nano-sized CSO particles with strong chemical bonding to the g-C3N4 matrix, thereby significantly enhancing photocatalytic activity compared to composites made by standard methods.
proposals.method: We will first prepare ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets via thermal exfoliation of bulk melamine-derived g-C3N4. For the composite, we will explore a one-pot solvothermal method where the exfoliated g-C3N4 is dispersed in ethylene glycol. Cobalt acetate and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) will be used as Co and Si precursors, respectively. The influence of different amine additives (e.g., ethylenediamine, hexamethylenetetramine) as complexing and structure-directing agents, inspired by Salavati-Niasari's prior work, will be systematically studied to control CSO morphology and dispersion. The composites will be characterized by XRD, FESEM/HRTEM, XPS, FTIR, UV-Vis DRS, and BET. Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated via hydrogen evolution from a water-methanol solution under visible light.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust synthetic protocol for creating intimately coupled CSO/g-C3N4 heterostructures with optimized interfacial engineering. It will provide fundamental insights into how precursor chemistry and reaction conditions dictate co-catalyst dispersion and composite morphology. The resulting materials are expected to show substantially higher photocatalytic hydrogen production rates, contributing to the development of efficient, non-precious metal-based photocatalysts.
proposals.novelty: While CSO/g-C3N4 composites are known, the systematic application of tailored solvothermal precursor chemistry—specifically the use of amine modifiers to control CSO nucleation directly on exfoliated g-C3N4 nanosheets—for maximizing loading efficiency and interfacial contact is a novel and rational design strategy not extensively reported pre-2026.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Photocatalytic Degradation Pathways and Ecotoxicity of Eriochrome Black T and Eriochrome Red B using a Novel Graphene-Integrated Double Perovskite Nanocatalyst
proposals.motivation: The photocatalytic degradation of azo dyes like Eriochrome Black T (EBT) and Eriochrome Red B (ER) is a promising water treatment strategy, but incomplete degradation can generate intermediates more toxic than the parent compounds. Pre-2026 research often focuses solely on degradation efficiency, neglecting the identification of byproducts and their environmental risk, creating a critical knowledge gap for safe technological implementation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a tailored graphene-integrated double perovskite nanocatalyst will not only degrade ER and EBT efficiently but also direct the reaction pathway towards less toxic intermediates, and that the ecotoxicity of the solution will correlate with specific intermediate species identified during degradation.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a novel nanocatalyst by integrating exfoliated graphene oxide with a tailored Lu-based double perovskite (e.g., Lu2CrMnO6) using a sol-gel/combustion method. Photocatalytic degradation of ER and EBT will be monitored via UV-Vis and HPLC. Intermediates will be identified using LC-MS/MS and GC-MS. The ecotoxicity of the parent dyes and degradation mixtures at different time points will be assessed using pre-2026 standard bioassays with Daphnia magna and Aliivibrio fischeri, linking toxicity profiles to specific intermediates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a complete mechanistic understanding of the degradation pathways for two persistent azo dyes using an advanced nanocatalyst. It will directly link catalyst design to degradation byproduct profiles and establish a crucial correlation between photocatalytic performance and the reduction of ecological risk, moving beyond simple efficiency metrics.
proposals.novelty: While Prof. Salavati-Niasari's group has expertise in synthesizing novel perovskites for degradation, the systematic integration of graphene with a double perovskite for pathway control, coupled with parallel, time-resolved intermediate identification and ecotoxicity assessment for these specific dyes, represents a novel and comprehensive approach not reported in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Plasmon-Enhanced Dicobalt Orthosilicate/g-C3N4 Heterojunctions for High-Efficiency Visible-Light Photocatalytic Degradation of Organic Pollutants
proposals.motivation: Visible-light photocatalysis is crucial for sustainable water purification, but many heterojunction systems, like the previously studied dicobalt orthosilicate/g-C3N4 (CSO/CN), suffer from limited light absorption efficiency and charge carrier recombination. Incorporating plasmonic nanoparticles offers a pre-2026 established strategy to overcome these limitations by enhancing local electromagnetic fields and generating hot carriers, thereby boosting photocatalytic activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strategic integration of plasmonic silver (Ag) or gold (Au) nanoparticles into the CSO/CN heterojunction will significantly enhance its visible-light absorption via localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and improve charge separation, leading to a substantial increase in the degradation rate of organic dyes like methylene blue and rhodamine B.
proposals.method: We will synthesize CSO nanoparticles and g-C3N4 nanosheets via sol-gel and thermal condensation methods, respectively, following Salavati-Niasari's established protocols. Plasmonic Ag nanoparticles will be deposited onto the heterojunction surface using a photochemical reduction technique, allowing for controlled size and distribution. The nanocomposites will be characterized by XRD, FESEM, TEM, UV-Vis DRS, and PL spectroscopy. Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated by monitoring the degradation of model dyes under visible-light irradiation, with the plasmonic contribution decoupled through comparative kinetics and scavenger experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, high-performance photocatalytic material system by merging heterojunction engineering with plasmonics. It will deliver fundamental insights into the synergistic mechanisms—such as resonant energy transfer and hot electron injection—between plasmonic nanoparticles and a CSO/CN semiconductor interface. The findings will advance the design principles for next-generation plasmon-enhanced photocatalysts.
proposals.novelty: While plasmonic enhancement of common semiconductors (e.g., TiO2) is known, its application to a novel, less-explored double silicate-based heterojunction (CSO/CN) is unprecedented. The novelty lies in tailoring the interfacial structure and LSPR properties specifically to amplify the existing charge separation mechanism within this unique heterojunction system.

proposals.title: Single-Particle Photocatalytic Dynamics in Cobalt Silicate/g-C3N4 Heterojunctions: Probing Charge Separation and Reaction Kinetics via Single-Molecule Fluorescence Microscopy
proposals.motivation: Ensemble measurements of photocatalytic materials, such as the Co2SiO4/g-C3N4 (CSO/CN) nanocomposites developed by Salavati-Niasari's group, average over heterogeneous particle properties, obscuring the fundamental link between nanoscale structure and function. Understanding the intrinsic charge separation efficiency and interfacial reaction kinetics at the individual heterojunction level is critical for rationally designing superior photocatalysts for water purification. Single-particle studies, enabled by advanced optical microscopy, can directly reveal this hidden heterogeneity and identify the key descriptors of high-performance particles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within a population of CSO/CN heterojunction particles, a sub-population of 'super-catalyst' particles exists, exhibiting exceptionally long-lived charge separation and faster interfacial electron transfer rates, which correlate with specific morphological and structural features at the single-particle level.
proposals.method: We will synthesize CSO/CN heterojunction nanoparticles using established sol-gel and thermal condensation methods from the group's prior work. Individual particles will be spatially isolated on a glass substrate. Using a custom-built, wide-field epifluorescence microscope with total internal reflection (TIRF) illumination and an EMCCD camera, we will track the stochastic 'blinking' fluorescence of a model redox probe (resazurin to resorufin) catalyzed by single particles under 405 nm laser excitation. Temporal trajectories of fluorescence intensity will be analyzed via change-point detection algorithms (like Bayesian blocks) to extract on/off event durations (reporting on charge recombination lifetimes) and event frequencies (reporting on reaction kinetics). These dynamic parameters will be correlated with particle morphology via concurrent atomic force microscopy (AFM) or scanning electron microscopy (SEM) imaging of the same sample locations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative map of charge carrier dynamics and reaction kinetics heterogeneity within CSO/CN photocatalysts. It will establish a foundational methodology for single-particle photocatalysis applicable to other material systems. The findings will yield concrete design principles—linking synthesis parameters to single-particle performance metrics—for engineering next-generation heterojunction nanomaterials with maximized active sites.
proposals.novelty: While single-molecule fluorescence is established in biophysics, its application to study inorganic heterojunction photocatalysis at the single-particle level, particularly for cobalt silicate-based systems, is novel. This approach moves beyond ensemble averages to directly observe and quantify the performance distribution and dynamic processes that define photocatalytic efficiency.

proposals.title: Probing Active Sites and Charge Transfer Pathways in Graphene-Based Heterojunctions via Dye-Selective Photocatalytic Degradation Profiling
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, while heterojunction photocatalysts like those combining graphene with perovskites or metal oxides show enhanced activity, the specific roles of different active sites (e.g., defect sites, heterojunction interfaces) and the dominant charge transfer pathways remain ambiguous. Conventional characterization techniques often require complex, ex-situ analysis and may not reflect dynamic, in-situ conditions during the photocatalytic reaction. A method to directly probe these properties through the reaction itself is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: The degradation kinetics and pathways of structurally distinct organic dyes (varying in charge, functional groups, and oxidation potentials) will serve as selective molecular probes, revealing the nature of reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, the relative contribution of oxidation vs. reduction sites, and the efficiency of charge separation at the heterojunction interface.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of graphene-based heterojunctions (e.g., g-C3N4/Co2SiO4/graphene, Lu2CrMnO6/graphene) using Prof. Salavati-Niasari's established sol-gel and hydrothermal techniques. Their photocatalytic degradation profiles will be meticulously tracked for a panel of dyes: cationic (methylene blue, rhodamine B), anionic (methyl orange, Congo red), and neutral (phenol). Kinetic analysis (pseudo-first-order rate constants), degradation intermediate identification via LC-MS, and scavenger experiments (for •OH, h+, •O2-) will be correlated for each dye-catalyst pair. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) of the multi-dye kinetic dataset will be used to classify catalyst behavior and link it to physicochemical properties from XRD, XPS, and PL spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, reaction-driven diagnostic framework for photocatalyst characterization, moving beyond simple efficiency metrics. It will provide direct, mechanistic insights into the structure-activity relationships of complex heterojunctions, guiding the rational design of next-generation catalysts. The methodology will be a valuable tool for the broader catalysis community to deconvolute contributions from different active sites.
proposals.novelty: Instead of using dyes merely as performance indicators, we invert the paradigm to use them as deliberate spectroscopic probes of the catalyst's electronic and surface properties. The systematic use of a diverse dye panel combined with multivariate analysis to fingerprint heterojunction behavior is a non-obvious and powerful approach beyond standard single-dye degradation tests.

proposals.title: Solar-Driven Photocatalytic Synthesis of Benzaldehyde from Benzyl Alcohol Using a Cobalt Silicate/Graphitic Carbon Nitride (CSO/CN) Heterojunction
proposals.motivation: Photocatalysis is predominantly applied to degradation processes, but the sustainable synthesis of high-value chemicals using solar energy remains a significant challenge. The selective oxidation of benzyl alcohol to benzaldehyde, a key industrial precursor, typically requires harsh conditions and stoichiometric oxidants. A photocatalytic route using water as a mild oxidant would offer a greener alternative, aligning with the need for sustainable chemical manufacturing.
proposals.hypothesis: The CSO/CN heterojunction, previously optimized for dye degradation, can be repurposed as an efficient and selective photocatalyst for the oxidation of benzyl alcohol to benzaldehyde under visible light, due to its enhanced charge separation and suitable band positions for activating both the organic substrate and water.
proposals.method: We will synthesize the CSO/CN nanocomposite via a hydrothermal method followed by calcination, as established in our prior work. The catalyst will be characterized by XRD, FESEM, TEM, UV-Vis DRS, and PL spectroscopy. Photocatalytic synthesis will be performed in a batch reactor under visible-light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) using benzyl alcohol in an aqueous acetonitrile solution, with nitrogen purging. Reaction products will be quantified by GC-MS and HPLC to determine conversion and selectivity, with controlled experiments to identify the active oxidative species.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate the dual functionality of a known degradation photocatalyst for synthetic chemistry, expanding the application scope of perovskite-derived nanocomposites. It will provide fundamental insights into the structure-activity relationship for selective oxidation, contributing to the design principles for 'versatile' photocatalysts in green chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While CSO/CN composites are known for degradation, their application to the selective synthesis of fine chemicals is unexplored. This repurposing of a catalyst from a 'degradation' to a 'synthesis' role for a specific, valuable transformation represents a novel conceptual shift in the field of photocatalysis.

proposals.title: Investigating Competitive Adsorption and Reaction Pathways in a Ternary Pollutant System Using a Graphene-Enhanced Perovskite Nanocomposite Photocatalyst
proposals.motivation: Industrial wastewater often contains complex mixtures of organic dyes, inorganic salts, and other organics, which can severely inhibit photocatalytic degradation efficiency compared to single-pollutant model systems. Pre-2026 research by Salavati-Niasari and others has excelled in synthesizing novel nanostructures and testing them on individual dyes, but their performance in realistic, multi-component streams remains poorly understood. This gap limits the practical application of advanced photocatalysts for real-world water remediation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a mixed pollutant stream (e.g., containing a cationic dye, an anionic dye, and a common salt like NaCl), competitive adsorption on the photocatalyst surface and radical scavenging by salt ions will dictate the overall degradation kinetics, with the graphene component mitigating inhibition by enhancing charge separation and providing additional adsorption sites.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a graphene oxide (GO)-integrated double perovskite nanocomposite (e.g., inspired by Lu2CrMnO6) via a sol-gel or hydrothermal method, leveraging the group's expertise in amine-assisted morphology control. Photocatalytic performance will be systematically evaluated under visible light using a ternary aqueous mixture containing methylene blue (MB), methyl orange (MO), and NaCl at varying concentrations. We will employ UV-Vis spectroscopy to monitor degradation kinetics, photoluminescence and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy to probe charge carrier dynamics, and FTIR analysis of spent catalysts to study adsorption preferences and potential intermediate formation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic understanding of inhibition and synergy effects in complex pollutant matrices, moving beyond single-pollutant studies. It will establish design principles for photocatalysts robust against realistic wastewater conditions and deliver quantitative data on the role of graphene in mitigating competitive inhibition, directly informing scale-up strategies.
proposals.novelty: While many studies test novel catalysts on dye mixtures, the systematic deconvolution of inhibition mechanisms—combining competitive adsorption analysis with ionic strength effects and in-situ charge carrier probing for a graphene-perovskite system—is novel. This approach directly addresses the 'complexity gap' between laboratory synthesis and practical application.

proposals.title: Magnetically Separable CoFe2O4/Graphene Nanocomposite for Piezo-Enhanced Catalytic Degradation of Organic Pollutants Under Dark Conditions
proposals.motivation: Photocatalysis is limited by its reliance on visible light, which is ineffective in turbid water or at night, hindering continuous wastewater treatment. Pre-2026 research by Salavati-Niasari's group on magnetic nanostructures and graphene composites for catalysis provides a foundation to explore alternative energy sources. Coupling magnetic recovery with mechanical energy activation (e.g., from water flow or ultrasound) could enable efficient, round-the-clock pollutant degradation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating piezocatalytic cobalt ferrite (CoFe2O4) nanoparticles with conductive graphene sheets will create a magnetically recoverable nanocomposite that efficiently generates reactive oxygen species under mechanical agitation (e.g., ultrasonication), enabling significant dye degradation in the absence of light.
proposals.method: We will synthesize CoFe2O4 nanoparticles via a sol-gel method, as per the group's expertise in oxide nanostructures, and anchor them onto reduced graphene oxide (rGO) sheets via a hydrothermal process. The nanocomposite will be characterized using XRD, FESEM, TEM, and VSM. Piezocatalytic activity will be evaluated by degrading methylene blue and rhodamine B in an ultrasonic bath (40 kHz, 200 W) in complete darkness, with the magnetic catalyst recovered using an external magnet for reuse studies. Control experiments with individual components and under static conditions will isolate the piezo-enhancement effect.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a proof-of-concept for a light-independent, magnetically separable catalytic system for water purification. It will contribute new knowledge on the synergy between piezoelectric materials and graphene for mechanical energy harvesting and establish a methodology for evaluating piezocatalytic performance in dark conditions, expanding the toolbox for advanced oxidation processes.
proposals.novelty: While the group has studied magnetic and photocatalytic materials, applying their CoFe2O4 synthesis expertise to create a graphene-based composite specifically for piezocatalysis in the dark is a novel direction. The integration aims to overcome the fundamental limitation of light dependence, which is a non-obvious extension of their prior visible-light photocatalyst work.

proposals.title: Homogeneous Photocatalytic Degradation of Organic Pollutants Using Soluble Bismuth-Iron Complexes: A Molecular Alternative to Solid Nanocatalysts
proposals.motivation: Heterogeneous photocatalysts, including nanostructured perovskites and graphene composites, face limitations in mass transfer, light penetration, and active site accessibility, which can hinder degradation efficiency. Homogeneous molecular catalysts offer a promising alternative by providing uniform dispersion and tunable electronic properties for enhanced light absorption and charge transfer. Developing soluble, earth-abundant metal complexes for photocatalytic water treatment addresses a critical need for more efficient and scalable degradation systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that soluble molecular complexes based on bismuth and iron, synthesized via ligand engineering, will exhibit superior photocatalytic activity for dye degradation under visible light compared to conventional solid-state catalysts, due to improved charge separation and reactant interaction in a homogeneous phase.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of soluble Bi/Fe complexes using amine-based (e.g., ethylenediamine, diethylenetriamine) and carboxylate ligands via solvothermal and co-precipitation techniques, drawing on the group's expertise. The complexes will be characterized using XRD, FT-IR, UV-Vis DRS, and photoluminescence spectroscopy to assess structure, optical properties, and charge carrier dynamics. Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated by monitoring the degradation of model organic dyes (e.g., methylene blue, rhodamine B) under visible-light irradiation, with kinetics compared to reference solid catalysts like graphitic carbon nitride nanocomposites.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of homogeneous photocatalysts based on non-toxic, abundant metals, expanding the toolkit for advanced oxidation processes. It will provide fundamental insights into the structure-activity relationship of molecular complexes in photocatalysis, bridging materials chemistry and coordination chemistry. The results could lead to more efficient, designer catalytic systems for environmental remediation.
proposals.novelty: While Prof. Salavati-Niasari's group has extensively studied solid nanostructures (e.g., double perovskites, nanocomposites), shifting to soluble molecular Bi/Fe complexes for homogeneous photocatalysis is a novel direction. The deliberate combination of bismuth and iron in a molecular framework to leverage their complementary optical and redox properties for degradation is unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Dual-Function Z-Scheme Heterojunction of Lu2CrMnO6/G-C3N4/RGO for Simultaneous Photocatalytic Pollutant Degradation and Hydrogen Evolution Under Visible Light
proposals.motivation: Addressing water pollution and clean energy scarcity requires efficient, multifunctional photocatalytic systems. Current visible-light photocatalysts often excel at either organic pollutant degradation or hydrogen evolution, but rarely both simultaneously, limiting process efficiency and practical application. Developing a single composite that can harness photogenerated charges for both oxidative degradation and reductive proton reduction represents a significant advancement in solar energy conversion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a direct Z-scheme heterojunction between a double perovskite (Lu2CrMnO6) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), further integrated with reduced graphene oxide (RGO), will create spatially separated redox sites. This architecture will simultaneously and efficiently drive the oxidation of organic pollutants and the reduction of water to hydrogen under visible light irradiation.
proposals.method: Lu2CrMnO6 nanoparticles will be synthesized via a sol-gel method using citric acid as a chelating agent, optimizing calcination temperature for crystallinity. g-C3N4 will be prepared by thermal polycondensation of melamine. An in-situ growth and self-assembly technique will be used to form the intimate Z-scheme heterojunction, followed by incorporation of RGO via hydrothermal reduction to enhance charge separation and provide conductive pathways. The composites will be characterized by XRD, FESEM, TEM, XPS, UV-Vis DRS, and PL spectroscopy. Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated by monitoring the degradation of methylene blue and hydrogen evolution from a water-methanol mixture under visible light (λ > 420 nm).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, rationally designed Z-scheme photocatalyst specifically engineered for dual redox functions. It will provide fundamental insights into charge carrier dynamics at the interface of double perovskites and carbon nitrides for concurrent oxidation and reduction. The study aims to establish a material design principle for integrated photocatalytic systems for environmental remediation and energy production.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the first-time integration of a Lu-based double perovskite, known for electrochemical hydrogen storage, into a Z-scheme with g-C3N4 for photocatalytic H2 production coupled with pollutant degradation. The deliberate use of RGO not as a mere support but as a charge mediator to facilitate the Z-scheme mechanism for this dual-purpose application is a non-obvious design.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Azo Dyes for Optimal Photocatalytic Degradation by BiFeO3/Bi25FeO40 Heterojunctions: A Combined DFT and Experimental Study
proposals.motivation: Photocatalyst development typically focuses on material optimization for degrading common pollutants. This forward approach is inefficient for complex industrial effluents. An inverse strategy, designing pollutant molecules to match a catalyst's specific electronic properties, could revolutionize targeted wastewater treatment and provide a fundamental test of degradation mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: By computationally tailoring the frontier molecular orbital energies and functional groups of azo dye molecules to align with the valence and conduction band positions of the BiFeO3/Bi25FeO40 heterostructure, we can design novel dyes that are degraded with significantly higher efficiency and selectivity than commercial dyes.
proposals.method: First, the band structure and density of states of hydrothermally synthesized BiFeO3/Bi25FeO40 will be characterized via UV-Vis DRS and valence band XPS. Using Density Functional Theory (DFT), we will then design a library of azo dye variants by systematically modifying auxochromes, anchoring groups, and conjugation length. The Gaussian 09 package with B3LYP functional and 6-311G(d,p) basis set will be used to calculate HOMO-LUMO levels and electrostatic potential maps. Dyes with optimal band alignment for charge transfer will be synthesized and their degradation kinetics under visible light assessed using UV-Vis spectroscopy and TOC analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel inverse design paradigm for pollutant-catalyst pairing, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will yield fundamental insights into the structure-activity relationship for dye degradation on perovskite heterojunctions and produce a set of 'model pollutant' molecules for standardized catalyst testing.
proposals.novelty: While catalyst design for dye degradation is common, the intentional molecular design of a pollutant to be optimally destroyed by a specific, known catalyst is a fundamentally inverted and unexplored approach. It directly leverages pre-2026 expertise in DFT and nanocomposite synthesis to solve the degradation problem from the opposite direction.

proposals.title: Scalable Hydrothermal Synthesis of Graphene-Integrated Fe-Mn Oxide Nanocomposites with Engineered Redox Stability for Dynamic Water Treatment
proposals.motivation: Iron-manganese oxides are effective natural sorbents for heavy metals, but their application in engineered water treatment is limited by reductive dissolution under fluctuating redox conditions, leading to contaminant remobilization. Pre-2026 research on scalable nanomaterial synthesis for water purification often prioritizes initial adsorption capacity over long-term stability in complex, real-world environments like stormwater ponds or mining-influenced waters. Developing a material whose synthesis is explicitly tuned for redox resilience is a critical step toward deployable environmental technology.
proposals.hypothesis: That the structural integration of Fe and Mn oxide phases, combined with conductive carbon networks (e.g., graphene or graphitic carbon nitride), during a scalable hydrothermal synthesis can be controlled to yield nanocomposites with exceptional resistance to reductive dissolution and metal leaching under oscillating redox potentials.
proposals.method: We will employ a scalable, amine-assisted hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis (inspired by Salavati-Niasari's work on Ni6MnO8 and double perovskites) to co-precipitate Fe-Mn oxide nanostructures with varied Fe:Mn ratios and crystallinity. Graphitic carbon nitride or reduced graphene oxide will be integrated in-situ to enhance conductivity and structural stability. Synthesis parameters (precursor type, pH, temperature, amine template) will be systematically varied. The key performance benchmark will be the material's stability during cyclic redox testing in a bespoke reactor, simulating conditions in managed aquifer recharge, monitored via metal leaching and phase transformations using XRD, XPS, and TEM.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable synthesis protocol for a new class of redox-stable nanocomposite sorbents, moving beyond lab-scale adsorption studies. It will establish explicit synthesis-stability relationships and deliver a material prototype suitable for pilot-scale trials in environments with dynamic water chemistry, directly addressing a major barrier to the field application of nanomaterial-based remediation.
proposals.novelty: The core innovation is defining 'performance' for scale-up by prioritizing redox resilience over just adsorption capacity, and explicitly tuning synthesis parameters to achieve this. This shifts the focus from studying a natural process to creating an engineered, environmentally robust technology based on that process.

proposals.title: Spectral Engineering of Perovskite-Graphene Nanohybrids for Synergistic Photocatalytic Detoxification of Co-contaminated Petrochemical Wastewaters
proposals.motivation: The remediation of petrochemical waste sites, often co-contaminated with persistent organic compounds (e.g., BTEX from gasoline) and toxic heavy metals (e.g., Pb, Cr), remains a critical environmental challenge. Conventional photocatalytic treatments, typically optimized for single pollutants under UV light, fail to address the complex interactions in mixed waste streams, where the degradation of organics can inadvertently alter metal mobility and toxicity. A fundamental understanding of how the incident light spectrum governs these coupled redox processes is essential for designing effective solar-driven remediation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tailoring the light spectrum (UV-only, visible-only, full solar) will selectively activate specific redox pathways in a tailored nanocatalyst, enabling the synergistic degradation of organic pollutants and the concurrent immobilization or recovery of heavy metals, rather than creating antagonistic effects.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a novel nanohybrid photocatalyst by integrating a double perovskite (e.g., Lu2CrMnO6, known for its redox-active sites and visible-light response) with reduced graphene oxide (rGO) to enhance charge separation and adsorption capacity. The photocatalytic activity will be evaluated against a model co-contaminant system (e.g., toluene and hexavalent chromium) in aqueous suspension under precisely controlled spectral conditions using bandpass filters. We will employ techniques like XRD, FESEM, and UV-Vis DRS for material characterization, while monitoring organic degradation via GC-MS and metal speciation/adsorption via ICP-OES and XPS.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental correlation between the excitation spectrum and the coupled degradation-immobilization efficiency for complex wastes. It will provide a design principle for 'spectrum-adaptive' photocatalytic systems and deliver a high-performance nanohybrid material tailored for the simultaneous detoxification of mixed petrochemical pollutants.
proposals.novelty: While perovskite and graphene-based photocatalysts have been studied for individual pollutant removal, the systematic investigation of spectral control as a tool to manage the synergistic redox fate of co-existing organic and inorganic contaminants is a novel and non-obvious strategy for complex waste remediation.

proposals.title: Site-Specific CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 Nanocomposite Sensor Arrays for High-Resolution Spatial Mapping of Heavy Metal Contamination at Lagos Gasoline Stations
proposals.motivation: Urban heavy metal pollution, particularly from leaded gasoline legacy and industrial activity, poses a severe, spatially heterogeneous threat to public health in megacities like Lagos. Current environmental monitoring relies on sparse, non-specific sampling, failing to capture localized contamination hotspots. There is a critical need for dense, low-cost sensor networks capable of generating high-fidelity spatial contamination maps to inform remediation and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the electrochemical sensing performance of CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 nanocomposites for heavy metals (e.g., Pb, Cd, Cu) can be systematically optimized through controlled synthesis parameters, and that deploying compositionally tailored variants across a city-scale network will yield a more accurate and actionable contamination profile than a single generic sensor.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 nanocomposites via a sol-gel and thermal condensation method, varying the precursor ratios, calcination temperature (300-600°C), and g-C3N4 exfoliation time. These will be characterized using XRD, FESEM, TEM, FT-IR, and BET. Their electrochemical sensing performance for heavy metals will be evaluated via cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Optimized variants will be deployed as screen-printed electrodes at selected gasoline stations, with data aggregated using a geospatial database to create contamination heatmaps.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, spatially resolved heavy metal contamination map for a critical urban environment. It will establish a direct synthesis-structure-performance relationship for CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 sensors in real-world matrices, moving beyond idealized lab conditions. The project will provide a scalable framework for integrating nanomaterial optimization with environmental screening.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop fusion of lab-based nanocomposite optimization with strategic, site-specific field deployment, creating a sensor network where material composition is a variable tuned to local environmental signatures, rather than using a uniform sensor array.

proposals.title: Solvothermal and Sonochemical Synthesis of PVDF/Ce³⁺ Nanocomposites for Enhanced Photocatalytic Degradation of Organic Pollutants
proposals.motivation: Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) nanocomposites with rare-earth dopants like Ce³⁺ are promising for photocatalytic water purification due to their stability and tunable optoelectronic properties. Pre-2026, electrospinning is the dominant, often sole, fabrication method for such fibrous nanocomposites, which limits structural diversity and scalability. Exploring alternative, solution-based synthetic routes is crucial to unlock different morphologies (e.g., hierarchical particles, dense films) that may offer superior charge separation, dye adsorption, and photocatalytic efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that solvothermal and sonochemical synthesis methods can produce PVDF/Ce³⁺ nanocomposites with distinct, non-fibrous nanostructures that exhibit superior photocatalytic activity compared to electrospun counterparts, due to enhanced crystallinity, better dopant incorporation, and higher active surface area.
proposals.method: We will employ two alternative synthesis techniques: (1) A solvothermal method, where PVDF and cerium(III) nitrate hexahydrate are dissolved in a DMF/acetone mixture and reacted in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 120-180°C to induce crystallization and composite formation. (2) A sonochemical method, using ultrasonic irradiation (20 kHz, 100 W) of a similar precursor solution to generate cavitation-induced nucleation and growth of Ce³⁺-incorporated PVDF nanoparticles. The synthesized materials will be characterized using XRD, FESEM-EDX, FTIR, UV-Vis DRS, and PL spectroscopy. Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated by monitoring the degradation of methylene blue and rhodamine B under visible-light irradiation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational comparison of synthesis routes for PVDF/rare-earth composites, directly linking non-electrospinning fabrication to material properties and photocatalytic function. It will provide new, scalable protocols for synthesizing these materials and identify optimal nanostructures for pollutant degradation, advancing the design of polymer-ceramic photocatalytic systems.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Salavati-Niasari's group has expertise in diverse nanostructure synthesis (e.g., perovskites, silicates), applying solvothermal and sonochemical methods specifically to PVDF/Ce³⁺ composites to circumvent the electrospinning paradigm is unexplored. The deliberate pursuit of non-fibrous morphologies for photocatalytic gain represents a significant departure from the established literature.

proposals.title: A Hydrophobic, Fluorescent, and Piezoelectric ZnO/Graphene/PDMS Nanocomposite Film for Dual-Mode Detection of Volatile Organic Compounds and Pressure Changes
proposals.motivation: The detection of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and mechanical stimuli is critical for environmental monitoring, industrial safety, and wearable electronics. Existing sensors often lack multifunctionality, environmental stability, or efficient signal transduction. Integrating hydrophobic, fluorescent, and piezoelectric properties into a single nanocomposite can enable robust, self-powered, and multi-responsive sensing platforms, addressing a significant pre-2026 materials challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a nanocomposite film integrating ZnO nanostructures (for piezoelectricity and fluorescence), graphene (for conductivity and quenching/recovery-based sensing), and a hydrophobic polymer matrix (e.g., PDMS) will exhibit a synergistic response, where VOC adsorption modulates fluorescence and piezoelectric output, enabling specific dual-mode sensing.
proposals.method: ZnO nanorods will be synthesized via a sol-gel method and decorated with reduced graphene oxide (rGO) using a hydrothermal process, as per Salavati-Niasari's expertise. This ZnO-rGO powder will be uniformly dispersed in a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) matrix to form a flexible film. The film's hydrophobic character will be confirmed by water contact angle measurements. Sensing prototypes will be fabricated by depositing the nanocomposite on interdigitated electrodes for resistive/fluorescence measurements and between conductive layers for piezoelectric output. VOC sensing will be tested by monitoring fluorescence quenching/recovery (using a spectrophotometer) and resistance changes upon exposure to analytes like ethanol and toluene. Piezoelectric response to pressure will be measured concurrently using a source meter.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, multifunctional sensor prototype that combines optical and electrical readouts with self-powering capability from mechanical stimuli. It will establish a structure-property relationship for hydrophobic nanocomposites in dual-mode sensing, advancing the design of robust environmental and wearable sensors beyond single-function devices.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate integration of three distinct physical properties (hydrophobicity, fluorescence, piezoelectricity) into one nanocomposite film specifically engineered for dual-mode (optical/electrical) sensing, a non-obvious combination not extensively explored in pre-2026 literature on graphene-based or perovskite-based sensors from this research group.

proposals.title: Probing the Degradation Mechanisms and Long-Term Stability of Cellulose-Graphene Oxide Hybrid Nanocomposites in Flexible Supercapacitors Under Cyclic and Environmental Stress
proposals.motivation: Flexible electronics and energy storage devices require robust, sustainable materials. Cellulose-graphene hybrids are promising due to their mechanical flexibility and conductivity, but their long-term performance in real-world conditions—subject to mechanical bending, humidity, and electrochemical cycling—remains poorly understood. Pre-2026 literature extensively reports initial synthesis and properties, but lacks systematic studies on operational degradation, which is critical for device reliability and commercialization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primary degradation pathways in cellulose-graphene oxide hybrids are interfacial delamination and chemical reduction of graphene oxide under operational stress, and that tailoring the covalent bonding and graphene oxide reduction degree will significantly enhance long-term stability.
proposals.method: We will synthesize hybrids via in-situ growth of cellulose on graphene oxide (GO) and reduced GO (rGO) using solvothermal and chemical precipitation techniques, drawing from the PI's expertise in nanostructure synthesis. Hybrid films will be fabricated into symmetric flexible supercapacitors. Stability will be assessed through accelerated aging tests: continuous galvanostatic charge-discharge cycling (10,000+ cycles), mechanical bending fatigue (10^5 cycles), and exposure to controlled humidity/temperature. Degradation will be monitored via electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, SEM/TEM for morphological changes, FTIR/XPS for chemical state analysis, and XRD for structural integrity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a detailed mechanistic understanding of failure modes in cellulose-graphene flexible devices. It will provide design rules for synthesizing more durable hybrid materials, directly contributing to the development of reliable, eco-friendly flexible electronics. The findings will bridge the gap between material synthesis and practical device engineering.
proposals.novelty: While many studies focus on initial photocatalytic or storage properties, this proposal uniquely applies rigorous, multi-stress degradation analysis—common in catalyst stability studies—to the operational stability of flexible energy storage devices, linking nanoscale interfacial chemistry directly to macroscopic device lifetime.

proposals.title: Cellulose-Graphene Hybrids as Sacrificial Catalytic Templates for the Synthesis of Novel Perovskite Nanostructures with Enhanced Photocatalytic Degradation Activity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the synthesis of complex metal oxide nanostructures, particularly perovskites and double perovskites, for photocatalytic degradation of pollutants remains challenging due to difficulties in controlling morphology and achieving high surface area. Simultaneously, cellulose-graphene hybrids are established as versatile, low-cost, and eco-friendly platforms but are primarily used directly as adsorbents or catalyst supports, not as active synthetic agents for creating other nanomaterials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cellulose-graphene oxide (CGO) hybrids can act as multifunctional sacrificial templates and catalysts, directing the nucleation and growth of novel perovskite-type metal oxides (e.g., derived from A2BB'O6 structures) with unique nanostructures, and that the resulting materials will exhibit superior photocatalytic activity for degrading persistent organic dyes under visible light.
proposals.method: We will synthesize cellulose-graphene oxide (CGO) hybrid aerogels via freeze-drying. These CGO hybrids will be immersed in aqueous precursor solutions containing metal salts (e.g., Co, Mn, Ni, Lu) tailored for target perovskites. The hybrid will act as a 3D scaffold, confining crystal growth. Subsequent calcination in air will sacrificially remove the cellulose and reduce GO to graphene, while simultaneously crystallizing the metal oxide. We will systematically vary the CGO ratio, precursor concentration, and calcination temperature to control the final nanostructure's phase, porosity, and graphene integration, characterizing them via XRD, FESEM, TEM, and BET.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, green synthetic route for complex metal oxide nanostructures using renewable hybrids. It will contribute fundamental understanding of how biopolymer-carbon composites can direct inorganic crystal growth. The resulting materials are expected to show enhanced photocatalytic degradation efficiency due to synergistic effects of high surface area, tailored morphology, and improved charge separation from integrated graphene.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in repurposing cellulose-graphene hybrids not as final composites, but as active, sacrificial 'reactors' for synthesizing entirely new classes of nanomaterials (e.g., double perovskites), a concept distinct from their conventional use as passive supports or adsorbents in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Size-Dependent Photocatalytic and Electrochemical Performance: A Comparative Study of Nanostructured vs. Microstructured DyMnO3/Dy2O3 Composites
proposals.motivation: The drive for efficient materials for environmental remediation and energy storage necessitates optimizing material architecture. While nanostructuring is widely believed to enhance activity due to increased surface area, a systematic, side-by-side comparison of nano- and micro-scale forms of the same rare-earth composite is lacking. This study directly addresses this gap for the promising DyMnO3/Dy2O3 system, providing empirical evidence to guide material design for photocatalysis and hydrogen storage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that nanostructured DyMnO3/Dy2O3 composites will exhibit superior photocatalytic degradation efficiency and electrochemical hydrogen storage capacity compared to their microstructured or bulk counterparts, primarily due to enhanced surface-to-volume ratio, improved charge carrier kinetics, and reduced diffusion pathways.
proposals.method: We will synthesize DyMnO3/Dy2O3 composites via a controlled sol-gel method (for nanostructures) and a conventional solid-state reaction (for microstructures). Morphology and phase purity will be characterized using FESEM, TEM, XRD, and XPS. Photocatalytic activity will be evaluated by monitoring the degradation of methylene blue and rhodamine B dyes under visible light. Electrochemical performance will be assessed via cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charge-discharge, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy to determine hydrogen storage capacity and kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative analysis of the 'nanoscale effect' for Dy-based perovskite/oxide composites, linking specific structural features to functional performance metrics. It will establish clear synthesis-structure-property relationships, offering a validated blueprint for designing high-activity materials for dual applications in pollutant degradation and energy storage.
proposals.novelty: While nanostructuring is common, a controlled comparative study of identical chemical compositions (DyMnO3/Dy2O3) across different size regimes for concurrent photocatalytic and electrochemical hydrogen storage applications is novel. This dual-functional approach directly tests the generality of the nanoscale advantage within a single, coherent experimental framework.

proposals.title: Structural Determinants in Photocatalytic Degradation: A Comparative Mechanistic Study of Acid Violet 7 and Structurally Diverse Azo Dyes Using Perovskite-Graphene Nanocomposites
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic degradation studies often generalize mechanistic pathways from model dyes like Acid Violet 7 to entire dye classes, potentially overlooking critical structure-activity relationships. This oversimplification hinders the rational design of catalysts for real-world wastewater containing diverse, complex pollutants. A systematic investigation comparing degradation intermediates and kinetics across structurally varied dyes is essential to advance predictive, application-driven photocatalysis.
proposals.hypothesis: The degradation pathway and efficiency for Acid Violet 7 are not generic but are significantly influenced by specific molecular features of the dye, such as the number and position of sulfonate groups, azo bond linkage, and aromatic ring substitution. We hypothesize that these structural differences will lead to distinct intermediate profiles and degradation rates when using the same catalyst.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of well-defined nanocomposites, building on the researcher's expertise, such as a double perovskite (e.g., analogous to Lu2CrMnO6) supported on reduced graphene oxide (rGO) for enhanced charge separation. Using this standardized catalyst, we will perform comparative photocatalytic degradation under visible light on Acid Violet 7 and selected azo dyes (e.g., Methyl Orange, Congo Red, Acid Red 88) differing in functional groups and complexity. Degradation kinetics will be monitored via UV-Vis spectroscopy, while intermediate species will be identified using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) and total organic carbon (TOC) analysis to assess mineralization extent.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, mechanistic framework linking dye molecular structure to degradation pathways, moving beyond generic efficiency reports. It will yield critical insights for tailoring perovskite-graphene catalysts for specific pollutant classes and establish a more rigorous protocol for evaluating photocatalytic performance in environmental remediation.
proposals.novelty: While many studies report dye degradation efficiency, a direct, detailed comparative analysis of mechanistic pathways for a homologous series of dyes using a single, advanced nanocomposite catalyst is lacking. This approach challenges the common assumption of a universal degradation mechanism by explicitly probing the influence of molecular structure.

proposals.title: Scalable Sonochemical Synthesis of Dicobalt Orthosilicate-g-C3N4 Nanocomposites: Reactor Design and Kilogram-Scale Photocatalytic Performance for Industrial Water Treatment
proposals.motivation: The laboratory-scale synthesis of advanced nanocomposite photocatalysts, such as the promising dicobalt orthosilicate-graphitic carbon nitride (Co2SiO4/g-C3N4), is well-established for degrading organic pollutants. However, a critical bottleneck preventing their industrial adoption is the lack of reliable, reproducible, and scalable synthesis methods capable of producing large quantities (grams to kilograms) without compromising the nanostructural properties that confer high activity. Addressing this scale-up challenge is essential to transition these materials from academic research to real-world environmental remediation applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing and optimizing a continuous-flow sonochemical reactor with controlled cavitation zones and precursor injection rates, we can achieve the scalable, gram-to-kilogram production of Co2SiO4/g-C3N4 nanocomposites while preserving the intimate heterojunction, high surface area, and defect structure responsible for their superior visible-light photocatalytic activity.
proposals.method: The project will involve the design and construction of a pilot-scale, continuous-flow sonochemical reactor equipped with multiple high-power ultrasonic transducers (20 kHz) arranged in series. Precursor solutions for Co2SiO4 and a dispersion of pre-synthesized g-C3N4 nanosheets will be pumped through the reactor at controlled rates. Key parameters for optimization include ultrasonic power density, flow rate, residence time, and pH, which will be monitored in real-time. The synthesized nanocomposite will be characterized using XRD, FESEM/EDS, TEM, BET, UV-Vis DRS, and PL spectroscopy to correlate synthesis conditions with structural, optical, and morphological properties. The photocatalytic efficiency of the scaled-up product will be benchmarked against lab-scale batches using methylene blue and rhodamine B degradation under visible light.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, scalable synthesis protocol for a high-performance nanocomposite photocatalyst, directly addressing the materials supply challenge for pilot-scale water treatment studies. It will establish critical process-structure-property relationships for sonochemical synthesis at scale, offering a generalizable framework for scaling other metal silicate or perovskite-based nanocomposites. The resulting kilogram quantities of catalyst will enable field trials and life-cycle assessment studies, bridging a significant gap between materials science and environmental engineering.
proposals.novelty: While sonochemistry is known for nanomaterial synthesis, its application for the continuous, large-scale production of complex heterostructured photocatalysts like Co2SiO4/g-C3N4 is unexplored. The novelty lies in the integrated reactor design and process optimization specifically tailored to co-synthesize and couple two distinct semiconductor phases in a single flow-through step, maintaining nanoscale interfacial contact crucial for charge separation.

proposals.title: DyMnO3/Dy2O3 Nanocomposite as a Selective Optical Probe for Ultrasensitive Detection of Cationic Organic Dyes in Water
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, monitoring trace organic pollutants, especially synthetic dyes in wastewater, is critical for environmental protection and public health. Existing colorimetric sensors often lack selectivity and sensitivity for specific dye classes. Leveraging the known optical and surface properties of rare-earth-based nanocomposites for a sensing, rather than degradation, application addresses a significant gap in rapid, on-site water quality analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: The unique surface chemistry and optical properties of the DyMnO3/Dy2O3 nanocomposite will enable selective and reversible adsorption of specific dye molecules (e.g., methylene blue, rhodamine B), leading to measurable and quantifiable changes in its UV-Vis diffuse reflectance or photoluminescence spectra proportional to dye concentration.
proposals.method: We will synthesize the DyMnO3/Dy2O3 nanocomposite via a sol-gel or hydrothermal method, optimizing parameters for high surface area and phase purity (confirmed by XRD, FESEM, TEM). The optical response (UV-Vis DRS, PL) of the nanocomposite powder to incremental additions of target dyes in aqueous solution will be systematically recorded. Selectivity will be tested against anionic dyes and common ions. Data will be analyzed using Langmuir/Freundlich isotherm models and calibration curves will be constructed for quantification, with detection limits calculated using 3σ/slope method.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, low-cost optical sensing platform based on a lanthanide perovskite/oxide composite for dye detection. It will provide fundamental insights into dye-composite surface interactions and demonstrate a practical 'inverse' application of photocatalytic materials, moving beyond their traditional use in degradation towards sensitive analytical probing.
proposals.novelty: While DyMnO3/Dy2O3 composites have been explored for catalysis, their application as a selective optical probe for dye sensing is unexplored. The 'inverse problem' approach—using a material's interaction with pollutants for detection instead of destruction—represents a novel conceptual shift within the field of environmental nanomaterials.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Steering of Photocatalytic Pathways in CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 Heterojunctions: A Systematic Synthesis-Characterization-Performance Mapping
proposals.motivation: While composite photocatalysts like CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 show promise for pollutant degradation, their optimization is largely empirical, focusing on a single performance metric. The fundamental charge transfer mechanism (e.g., type-II heterojunction vs. Z-scheme), which dictates reaction selectivity and efficiency, remains poorly understood and is rarely the deliberate target of synthesis. A systematic, mechanism-centric design strategy is needed to move beyond trial-and-error towards rational catalyst engineering for specific redox reactions.
proposals.hypothesis: The dominant charge transfer pathway in CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 composites can be deliberately steered from a conventional type-II to a direct Z-scheme mechanism by precisely controlling the mass ratio and interfacial synthesis conditions, thereby optimizing the system for specific photocatalytic reactions requiring strong redox potentials.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a matrix of CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 composites via a hydrothermal-calcination method, varying mass ratios (5-95 wt% g-C3N4), temperature (120-180°C), and time. Each sample will be evaluated for photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue and tetracycline under visible light. Advanced in-situ and ex-situ characterization, including in-situ XPS under illumination, ESR for •O2−/•OH radical detection, radical trapping experiments, and photodeposition of Pt and PbO2, will be correlated with performance data to map the operative charge transfer mechanism across the synthesis parameter space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, experimentally-verified map linking synthesis parameters to charge transfer mechanisms and photocatalytic function. It will establish a generalizable methodology for the rational design of composite photocatalysts, moving the field from performance optimization to mechanistic engineering. The resulting guidelines will enable the targeted creation of catalysts optimized for specific reactions, such as heavy-duty oxidation or hydrogen evolution.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior studies that optimize composites for a single performance output, this work treats the charge transfer mechanism itself as the primary optimization target. The systematic, high-resolution mapping of synthesis conditions to a definitive mechanistic outcome using a suite of in-situ techniques represents a significant shift from descriptive to predictive materials design in photocatalysis.

proposals.title: A Robust rGO-Scaffolded g-C3N4/CuMn2O4 Ternary Hybrid for Enhanced Visible-Light Photocatalytic Degradation of Organic Pollutants
proposals.motivation: The practical application of semiconductor-based photocatalysts is severely limited by poor structural stability, rapid charge recombination, and limited active sites. While synergistic hybrids like g-C3N4 with spinel oxides (e.g., CuMn2O4) show promise for visible-light activity, their performance degrades due to particle aggregation and weak interfacial contact, hindering long-term use in water remediation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a ternary hybrid where exfoliated g-C3N4 nanosheets and CuMn2O4 nanoparticles are co-immobilized on a conductive reduced graphene oxide (rGO) scaffold will create a robust, integrated architecture that significantly enhances charge separation, provides mechanical stability, and boosts photocatalytic efficiency and durability.
proposals.method: The hybrid will be synthesized via a multi-step wet-chemical approach. First, CuMn2O4 nanoparticles will be prepared via a sol-gel method using metal nitrates and citric acid. Exfoliated g-C3N4 nanosheets will be obtained by thermal oxidation etching of bulk g-C3N4. These components will then be sequentially anchored onto a pre-synthesized rGO framework through ultrasonic-assisted self-assembly and hydrothermal treatment to ensure strong interfacial coupling. The structure, morphology, and optical properties will be characterized using XRD, FESEM/HRTEM, XPS, UV-Vis DRS, and PL spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, mechanically stable photocatalyst design that effectively mitigates charge recombination through the conductive rGO highway. It will provide a generalizable scaffold-based strategy for constructing durable multi-component catalytic systems and demonstrate superior, stable photocatalytic degradation performance for model organic dyes (e.g., methylene blue, rhodamine B) under visible light.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate use of rGO not just as an additive, but as a foundational, three-dimensional conductive scaffold that structurally integrates and stabilizes both a metal-free semiconductor (g-C3N4) and a bimetallic spinel oxide (CuMn2O4), creating a uniquely coupled and resilient ternary interface not previously reported for this combination.

proposals.title: Inverted Scaffold Design of a g-C3N4/CuMn2O4 Nanocomposite for Enhanced Visible-Light Photocatalytic Degradation of Organic Pollutants
proposals.motivation: Conventional inorganic-organic photocatalysts often rely on rigid inorganic frameworks as structural supports, which can limit charge carrier mobility and active site accessibility. Inspired by a paradigm shift in tissue engineering, where soft organic scaffolds are functionalized with inorganic nanoparticles, this work proposes a novel composite architecture.
