Adapting this 'scaffold reversal' to materials chemistry could unlock new pathways for charge separation and pollutant degradation, addressing the persistent challenge of efficient visible-light photocatalysts for water purification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a continuous, porous graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) network as a soft, photoresponsive scaffold, decorated with well-dispersed CuMn2O4 nanoparticles, will create superior interfacial charge transfer and synergistic redox activity. This inverted architecture will outperform traditional composites where the inorganic phase forms the structural backbone.
proposals.method: The g-C3N4 scaffold will be synthesized via thermal polycondensation of melamine, followed by ultrasonic exfoliation to create a porous 2D network. CuMn2O4 nanoparticles will be prepared via a sol-gel method using metal nitrates and citric acid, as per Salavati-Niasari's expertise in transition metal oxide synthesis. Decoration will be achieved through an in-situ deposition technique, where the pre-formed nanoparticles are anchored onto the g-C3N4 surface using linker molecules and subsequent calcination under controlled atmosphere. The composites will be characterized by XRD, FESEM, TEM, XPS, UV-Vis DRS, and PL spectroscopy, with photocatalytic activity evaluated via the degradation of methylene blue and rhodamine B under visible light.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new design principle for photocatalytic composites by demonstrating the efficacy of an inverted organic-inorganic scaffold. It is expected to yield a material with enhanced visible-light absorption, prolonged charge carrier lifetime, and high degradation efficiency. The findings will provide fundamental insights into the role of structural hierarchy in dictating photocatalytic performance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly challenging the conventional assumption that the inorganic component must be the structural backbone, by adapting a bio-inspired scaffold inversion. This approach is distinct from prior g-C3N4 or spinel oxide composites, where components are typically mixed or coated in a traditional hierarchy.

proposals.title: Machine Learning-Driven Synthesis of g-C3N4-Supported Perovskite/Spinel Nanodots for Enhanced S-Scheme Photocatalytic Hydrogen Evolution
proposals.motivation: S-scheme heterojunctions offer a promising route to enhance photocatalytic efficiency by preserving high redox potentials and promoting charge separation. However, the rational design of such systems, particularly the selection of optimal electron mediators like perovskites and spinels, remains largely empirical and time-consuming. Integrating machine learning with controlled synthesis can accelerate the discovery of novel, high-performance photocatalytic composites for sustainable energy applications like water splitting.
proposals.hypothesis: That machine learning models can accurately predict perovskite and spinel nanoparticles (e.g., CuMn2O4, LaFeO3) with optimal band structures to function as efficient electron mediators on a g-C3N4 nanosheet core, and that synthesizing these predicted nanoparticles directly onto the support in an inverted architecture will create an S-scheme system with superior charge dynamics and photocatalytic activity.
proposals.method: We will train a Gradient Boosting Regression model (e.g., XGBoost) on pre-2026 material databases (e.g., Materials Project) to predict band gaps and band edge positions of candidate perovskites and spinels. The top ML-predicted compositions will be synthesized directly onto pre-formed g-C3N4 nanosheets via a solvothermal or precipitation method, inverting the typical synthesis hierarchy. The resulting composites will be characterized using XRD, TEM, XPS, and UV-Vis DRS, with photocatalytic performance evaluated via hydrogen evolution under visible light. Experimental results will be fed back to iteratively refine the ML model.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel ML-guided framework for the targeted design of S-scheme photocatalysts, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will produce a new family of high-activity, g-C3N4-supported nanodot composites with validated charge transfer pathways. The iterative loop between prediction and experiment will also generate a refined dataset to advance computational materials discovery for photocatalysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop integration of ML prediction with the deliberate inversion of the composite synthesis hierarchy (growing mediator nanoparticles directly on the support), creating a non-conventional architecture to fundamentally probe and optimize S-scheme charge dynamics, which is distinct from typical layered or mixed heterojunctions.

proposals.title: Graphitic Carbon Nitride as a Core Support for CuMn2O4 Nanoparticles: An Inverted Hierarchical Composite for Enhanced Visible-Light Photocatalysis
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is typically employed as a co-catalyst or a secondary component decorated onto inorganic semiconductor supports (e.g., TiO2, perovskites) to enhance charge separation. This conventional hierarchy often limits the full exploitation of g-C3N4's high surface area and tunable surface chemistry as a primary scaffold. Inverting this relationship to use g-C3N4 as the core support for active inorganic nanoparticles could create novel interfacial architectures, potentially leading to superior photocatalytic activity for environmental remediation, a key pre-2026 challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using thermally exfoliated g-C3N4 nanosheets as a core support for uniformly dispersed CuMn2O4 spinel nanoparticles will create a more intimate and extensive heterojunction interface, leading to enhanced visible-light absorption, improved charge carrier separation, and superior photocatalytic degradation efficiency compared to composites where g-C3N4 is the secondary component.
proposals.method: Thermally exfoliated g-C3N4 nanosheets will be synthesized via direct pyrolysis of melamine followed by liquid exfoliation. CuMn2O4 nanoparticles will be anchored onto the g-C3N4 surface using a facile sol-gel/impregnation method, where a precursor solution of copper(II) nitrate and manganese(II) nitrate in ethanol is mixed with the g-C3N4 dispersion, followed by controlled calcination at 400°C in air. The morphology, structure, and optical properties will be characterized using FESEM, TEM, XRD, FT-IR, UV-Vis DRS, and PL spectroscopy. Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated by monitoring the degradation of methylene blue and tetracycline hydrochloride under visible-light irradiation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel composite design paradigm by demonstrating g-C3N4 as an effective core support for spinel oxides, moving beyond its traditional auxiliary role. It will provide fundamental insights into the charge transfer dynamics at the inverted inorganic-organic interface. The resulting material is expected to contribute significantly to the development of efficient, visible-light-driven photocatalysts for water purification.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in deliberately inverting the established structural hierarchy of g-C3N4/inorganic composites. While pre-2026 work by Salavati-Niasari and others often used g-C3N4 to decorate or be combined with inorganic phases, this proposal positions g-C3N4 unequivocally as the primary architectural scaffold, a non-obvious approach for optimizing interfacial contact and charge transport.

proposals.title: Beyond Visible Light: A Systematic Evaluation of UV vs. Full Solar Spectrum Irradiation on the Photocatalytic Degradation Efficiency of Tailored Perovskite-Graphene Nanocomposites
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, photocatalysis research, including Prof. Salavati-Niasari's work on perovskites and graphene composites, has heavily emphasized visible-light activation for solar energy utilization. However, the relative contribution of the UV component in natural sunlight, which carries higher photon energy, is often underappreciated in efficiency comparisons. A direct, controlled study comparing full-spectrum solar simulation to isolated UV irradiation is needed to determine the most effective energy input for rapid degradation of recalcitrant pollutants, informing practical reactor design and material optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for wide-bandgap or surface-modified perovskite-graphene nanocomposites, photocatalytic degradation rates under UV-only irradiation will significantly exceed those under visible-light-only and full solar spectrum conditions for specific pollutant classes, challenging the paradigm that visible light activation is universally optimal for solar-driven applications.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Lu2CrMnO6 double perovskite nanostructures, integrating them with graphitic carbon nitride and reduced graphene oxide via sol-gel and hydrothermal methods from the researcher's established toolkit. Photocatalytic efficiency will be tested for dye degradation (e.g., methylene blue, rhodamine B) under three rigorously controlled light sources: (1) a filtered xenon lamp simulating AM 1.5G full solar spectrum, (2) the same source with a UV-cutoff filter (>420 nm) for visible light only, and (3) a UV-LED source (λ~365 nm) for UV-only irradiation. Reaction kinetics will be monitored via UV-Vis spectroscopy, and charge carrier dynamics will be probed using photoluminescence and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative comparison of photocatalytic efficiency under different spectral regimes for advanced nanocomposites, directly linking material properties to operational performance. It will establish clear guidelines on whether maximizing UV response or broad-spectrum harvesting is more critical for degradation efficiency, impacting the design of future photocatalytic systems and testing protocols.
proposals.novelty: While many studies compare visible-light catalysts to UV-active benchmarks (e.g., TiO2), a controlled, side-by-side investigation of the same tailored nanocomposite under full-spectrum, visible-only, and UV-only irradiation to deconvolute their contributions is novel and non-obvious, moving beyond simple catalyst screening to fundamental energy-input optimization.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Diagnostic Array for Profiling the Versatility and Selectivity of a CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 S-Scheme Photocatalyst Against Diverse Aqueous Pollutants
proposals.motivation: The development of broad-spectrum photocatalysts for water remediation is critical, as real wastewater contains complex mixtures of pollutants from different classes. Pre-2026, catalyst testing is often sequential and pollutant-specific, which is inefficient for assessing practical versatility and fails to reveal potential for forming toxic intermediates across different contaminants. A systematic, parallel assessment method is needed to rapidly profile catalyst performance, akin to diagnostic panels used in clinical microbiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-throughput, multi-pollutant diagnostic array will reveal significant differences in the degradation efficiency and by-product formation of the CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 S-scheme photocatalyst across various pollutant classes, uncovering non-obvious selectivity and potential limitations not apparent in single-pollutant studies.
proposals.method: We will synthesize the CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 nanocomposite via Salavati-Niasari's established sol-gel and thermal polymerization methods. A standardized catalyst suspension will be dispensed into a 96-well photoreactor plate, where each column is pre-loaded with a different target pollutant (ciprofloxacin, atrazine, methylene blue, bisphenol A) in a consistent aqueous matrix. The plate will be irradiated under a uniform visible-light source (e.g., 300W Xe lamp with 420 nm cutoff filter). Degradation kinetics will be monitored in parallel via in-situ UV-Vis spectroscopy (using a plate reader), and intermediate formation will be tracked using HPLC-MS samples taken from each well at fixed time intervals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a standardized, high-throughput protocol for rapidly evaluating photocatalytic versatility, moving beyond single-pollutant studies. It will generate a comprehensive performance profile for the novel S-scheme catalyst, directly linking its properties to effectiveness against pharmaceuticals, pesticides, dyes, and endocrine disruptors. The data will critically inform the catalyst's practical suitability and highlight pollutant-specific degradation pathways and risks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual transfer of a clinical diagnostic array format to materials science for parallel catalyst screening, and in the simultaneous evaluation of a single photocatalyst's performance and by-product formation across multiple, chemically distinct pollutant classes under identical conditions.

proposals.title: Geochemically-Informed Assessment of a CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 S-Scheme Photocatalyst for Degradation of Pharmaceutical Mixtures in Simulated Real Water Matrices
proposals.motivation: Most photocatalytic degradation studies are performed in pure water, which fails to account for the complex inorganic background of real water bodies. This leads to an overestimation of catalyst performance and a poor understanding of real-world applicability, particularly for emerging S-scheme heterojunctions. Evaluating photocatalysts in geochemically representative matrices is crucial for translating lab-scale success to practical water remediation technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: The photocatalytic efficiency and selectivity of the CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 S-scheme heterojunction for degrading pharmaceutical mixtures will be significantly modulated by the specific inorganic ion composition of simulated local water, due to competitive adsorption, radical scavenging, and potential catalyst surface poisoning.
proposals.method: We will synthesize CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 nanocomposites via a sol-gel and thermal polycondensation method, followed by comprehensive characterization (XRD, FESEM, TEM, XPS, UV-Vis DRS, PL). Complex aqueous matrices will be formulated based on a pre-existing local geochemical baseline for 25 elements (e.g., Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl-, SO42-, HCO3-). Photocatalytic degradation of a model pharmaceutical mixture (e.g., ciprofloxacin, diclofenac) will be tested under visible light in these matrices versus deionized water, with kinetics monitored by HPLC-MS. Catalyst stability and surface changes post-reaction will be analyzed to identify deactivation mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, practical performance benchmark for an advanced S-scheme photocatalyst under environmentally relevant conditions. It will generate novel insights into how specific geochemical constituents influence photocatalytic pathways and catalyst longevity, moving the field beyond idealized single-pollutant studies.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a comprehensive local geochemical baseline to design complex test matrices for photocatalytic assessment is a novel, environmentally grounded approach. Systematically studying its impact on a CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 S-scheme system for mixed pollutants bridges materials science with environmental chemistry in a non-obvious way.

proposals.title: Expanding the Pollutant Scope of a CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 S-Scheme Heterojunction: Photocatalytic Degradation of Persistent Pharmaceuticals and Pesticides
proposals.motivation: The widespread contamination of water resources by persistent organic pollutants, such as pharmaceutical residues (e.g., antibiotics, analgesics) and pesticides, poses a severe threat to ecosystems and human health. Conventional water treatment methods often fail to remove these recalcitrant compounds efficiently. Photocatalysis, particularly using novel heterojunction systems, offers a promising advanced oxidation process for their complete mineralization, addressing a critical pre-2026 environmental challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 S-scheme photocatalyst, previously effective for dye degradation, will demonstrate superior and broad-spectrum photocatalytic activity against structurally diverse, persistent organic pollutants due to its enhanced charge separation, strong redox potential, and visible-light responsiveness.
proposals.method: The CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 nanocomposite will be synthesized via a pre-established sol-gel and thermal polycondensation method. Its physicochemical properties will be characterized using XRD, FESEM-EDS, TEM, FTIR, UV-Vis DRS, and PL spectroscopy. Photocatalytic performance will be systematically evaluated under visible-light irradiation against model pollutants: the antibiotic ciprofloxacin, the analgesic diclofenac, and the herbicide 2,4-D. Degradation kinetics and pathways will be monitored via HPLC-MS and TOC analysis, with active species identified through scavenger experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive assessment of the practical applicability of the CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 S-scheme system beyond model dyes. It will establish structure-activity relationships for degrading complex pollutants and contribute fundamental insights into the degradation mechanisms of pharmaceuticals and pesticides via this heterojunction, advancing the field of tailored photocatalysts for real wastewater treatment.
proposals.novelty: While S-scheme concepts and g-C3N4-based composites are known, the specific application of the CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 heterojunction to degrade a suite of high-concern, non-dye pollutants is unexplored. The study uniquely tests the catalyst's versatility and mechanistic efficacy against pollutants with distinct chemical structures and functional groups.

proposals.title: Optimization of a CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 Z-Scheme Heterojunction: Synergistic Effects of Mass Ratio and Synthesis Parameters on Photocatalytic Degradation Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, semiconductor heterojunctions, particularly Z-scheme systems like those involving g-C3N4, are heavily researched for visible-light photocatalysis to address water pollution. However, the performance of composites like spinel CuMn2O4 with g-C3N4 is highly sensitive to interfacial contact and charge transfer dynamics, which are dictated by the composite ratio and synthesis pathway. A systematic optimization of these parameters is lacking but critical for maximizing the synergistic effects and achieving practical application benchmarks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an optimal CuMn2O4 to g-C3N4 mass ratio exists, and that coupling this with a tailored in-situ hydrothermal synthesis will create an intimate Z-scheme heterojunction, significantly enhancing charge separation and visible-light photocatalytic activity for dye degradation compared to individually synthesized and physically mixed counterparts.
proposals.method: CuMn2O4 will be synthesized via a sol-gel method using copper and manganese nitrates and citric acid. g-C3N4 will be prepared by thermal polycondensation of melamine. Composites will be fabricated by an in-situ hydrothermal method, where pre-formed g-C3N4 is dispersed in the precursor solution for CuMn2O4. We will systematically vary the mass ratio (e.g., 1:9 to 9:1 CuMn2O4:g-C3N4) and key hydrothermal parameters (temperature: 120-180°C, time: 6-24 h). Performance will be evaluated by monitoring the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue and rhodamine B under visible light, correlated with structural (XRD, FT-IR), morphological (SEM, TEM), and optical (UV-Vis DRS, PL) characterization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a definitive protocol for synthesizing a high-performance CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 Z-scheme photocatalyst, providing clear structure-property relationships. It will contribute a validated, optimized material with superior degradation kinetics for organic pollutants, advancing the fundamental understanding of charge carrier dynamics in this specific spinel/nitride composite system.
proposals.novelty: While g-C3N4 composites are common, a systematic study simultaneously optimizing the mass ratio and in-situ hydrothermal growth conditions for a CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 Z-scheme system is novel. This approach moves beyond simple physical mixing or standard composite preparation, aiming to engineer the interface at the synthesis stage for optimal performance.

proposals.title: In-situ spectroscopic and morphological mapping of degradation pathways in perovskite and graphitic carbon nitride nanocomposites for robust photocatalytic water purification
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic degradation of water pollutants is promising, but practical application is hindered by catalyst deactivation during extended cycling. Pre-2026 literature often reports simple reusability tests without identifying the precise chemical or structural failure modes. A mechanistic understanding of deactivation is critical to design materials that maintain high activity over long operational lifetimes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the deactivation of advanced nanocomposite photocatalysts under extended cycling is not a singular process but results from the superposition of specific, identifiable pathways, including active site poisoning by recalcitrant intermediates, cation leaching, and photo-corrosion-induced phase changes.
proposals.method: We will synthesize model systems based on Prof. Salavati-Niasari's expertise: double perovskite (e.g., Lu2CrMnO6) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanocomposites. Long-term photocatalytic cycling for dye degradation will be coupled with in-situ/operando techniques: periodic FTIR and Raman spectroscopy to track surface adsorbates and structural integrity, XPS to monitor elemental leaching and oxidation states, and TEM/SEM to correlate morphological evolution. Controlled experiments will isolate stressors by varying pollutant type, pH, and light intensity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed 'degradation map' linking performance loss to specific physicochemical changes. It will move the field beyond empirical durability ratings to establish design rules for stability, such as protective surface functionalization or stable dopant incorporation. The findings will directly inform the synthesis of next-generation, robust photocatalysts.
proposals.novelty: While many studies report photocatalytic reusability, few systematically deconvolve the root causes of deactivation using a suite of in-situ techniques on advanced nanocomposites. This approach directly connects the synthesis expertise of the PI to a fundamental, mechanistic investigation of material failure.

proposals.title: Assessing the Long-Term Environmental Stability of Fe-Mn Oxide-Graphene Nanocomposites for Combined Photocatalysis and Metal Sequestration Under Cyclic Redox Stress
proposals.motivation: Photocatalysts for water remediation, especially those designed for dual functions like organic dye degradation and toxic metal immobilization, face a critical gap in stability assessment. Pre-2026 research, including by Salavati-Niasari's group, focuses on synthesis and initial performance but rarely tests material integrity under the dynamic, fluctuating redox conditions (oxic/anoxic cycles) prevalent in real soil and aquatic environments. This disconnect between lab-scale activity and long-term field utility limits practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that repeated redox cycling will induce phase transformations and structural degradation in Fe-Mn oxide-graphene photocatalysts, leading to a significant decline in both photocatalytic reusability and the stability of sequestered heavy metals (e.g., Pb, Cd) over multiple operational cycles.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Fe-Mn oxide nanostructures on reduced graphene oxide (rGO) sheets using a sol-gel method, building on the group's expertise with perovskite and silicate systems. The nanocomposite will undergo sequential stress cycles: each cycle comprises (1) a photocatalytic methylene blue degradation test under visible light, (2) an induced anoxic phase using N2 purging with a sodium dithionite solution to simulate reducing conditions, and (3) a return to oxic conditions. After defined cycles, the stability of pre-loaded metals will be quantified using a modified Tessier sequential extraction procedure, analyzing metal fractions from exchangeable to residual. Material characterization (XRD, XPS, TEM) will track morphological and phase changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, holistic protocol for evaluating the environmental durability of multifunctional nanomaterials, moving beyond simple reuse tests. It will yield critical structure-property-stability relationships for Fe-Mn-rGO systems under redox stress, directly informing their design for sustainable environmental applications. The data will bridge materials chemistry with environmental engineering perspectives.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated testing protocol that explicitly couples photocatalytic reuse with simulated geochemical redox fluctuations and quantifies the impact on a secondary function (metal sequestration) via sequential extraction—a combination not reported in pre-2026 photocatalyst stability studies.

proposals.title: Systematic Investigation of Long-Term Stability and Reusability of Graphitic Carbon Nitride-Based Nanocomposite Photocatalysts for Water Purification
proposals.motivation: While high initial photocatalytic activity is often reported for novel nanomaterials, their practical deployment in water treatment is hindered by a lack of data on long-term operational stability. Pre-2026 literature, including Prof. Salavati-Niasari's work on g-C3N4/dicobalt orthosilicate, frequently lacks rigorous, multi-cycle reusability testing under sustained irradiation. Assessing durability is critical to evaluate economic viability and identify degradation mechanisms that limit catalyst lifespan.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the integration of graphene or double perovskite nanostructures with g-C3N4 will significantly enhance its structural and chemical stability during prolonged photocatalytic cycling, mitigating common deactivation pathways like photocorrosion, active site poisoning, or particle aggregation.
proposals.method: We will synthesize g-C3N4 nanocomposites with graphene oxide and Lu2CrMnO6 double perovskite, following established sol-gel and hydrothermal techniques from the researcher's portfolio. The core method involves a standardized, automated cyclic degradation test of a model pollutant (e.g., methylene blue) under visible light, running for a minimum of 50 consecutive cycles. After each cycle, catalyst recovery via centrifugation will be followed by performance measurement and material characterization (XRD, FT-IR, XPS, TEM) at defined intervals to track morphological, compositional, and structural changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a benchmark dataset on the operational longevity of advanced g-C3N4 hybrids, directly informing their practical applicability. It will elucidate the correlation between nanocomposite architecture and specific deactivation mechanisms, offering guidelines for designing more robust photocatalysts. The findings will move the field beyond initial activity reports toward realistic performance assessments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, high-cycle-count stability protocol applied to these specific material classes, combined with in-depth mechanistic analysis timed with performance decay, which is far more extensive than the typical 4-5 cycle tests common in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Plasmonic Antenna-Receiver Coupling in CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 Nanocomposites for Enhanced Solar-Driven Photocatalytic Degradation
proposals.motivation: The efficiency of semiconductor photocatalysts like g-C3N4 is limited by poor visible-light absorption and rapid charge recombination. While plasmonic nanoparticles can enhance light harvesting, their integration often lacks a targeted design to focus the enhanced electromagnetic field directly at the catalytic interface, limiting gains in charge separation and photocatalytic activity.
proposals.hypothesis: By rationally designing plasmonic nanostructures (optical antennas) with geometries and compositions tuned to the solar spectrum, we can create intense, localized surface plasmon resonances that concentrate light specifically at the heterojunction interface of a CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 composite, thereby dramatically amplifying the local electric field and boosting photocatalytic reaction rates.
proposals.method: We will synthesize anisotropic plasmonic nanoparticles (e.g., Au nanorods, Ag nanocubes) via colloidal methods, tuning their aspect ratios to resonate across the visible spectrum. These will be integrated with a hydrothermally synthesized CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 heterostructure. Finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations will guide antenna geometry design to maximize the electric field enhancement at the composite interface. Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated by degrading model organic dyes (e.g., methylene blue, rhodamine B) under simulated solar irradiation, with detailed characterization of charge carrier dynamics via photoluminescence and photoelectrochemical measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new design principle for plasmon-enhanced photocatalysts by introducing a targeted antenna-receiver coupling mechanism. It will yield a highly efficient, solar-driven nanocomposite for environmental remediation and contribute fundamental insights into the spatial management of plasmonic near-fields for optimizing charge separation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual translation of RF antenna-receiver coupling to photocatalysis, moving beyond simple plasmonic doping to a rational, simulation-guided design where the plasmonic component is engineered specifically to pump the catalytic heterojunction interface.

proposals.title: Plasmonic Ag Nanoparticle-Decorated CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 Z-Scheme Heterojunctions for Enhanced Visible-Light Photocatalytic Degradation of Antibiotics
proposals.motivation: The photocatalytic degradation of persistent organic pollutants, such as antibiotics in wastewater, remains a significant challenge due to inefficient light absorption and rapid charge recombination in semiconductor catalysts. Pre-2026 research on composites like CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 shows promise for visible-light activity but is fundamentally limited by their optical and electronic properties. Integrating principles from nanophotonics offers a pathway to overcome these intrinsic material limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that decorating a Z-scheme CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 heterojunction with plasmonic silver nanoparticles will synergistically enhance photocatalytic performance by leveraging localized surface plasmon resonance to amplify light absorption and generate intense local electromagnetic fields, thereby boosting charge carrier generation and separation.
proposals.method: We will synthesize CuMn2O4 spinel nanoparticles via a sol-gel method and exfoliate g-C3N4 nanosheets from melamine. A Z-scheme heterojunction will be formed via ultrasonic-assisted assembly. Plasmonic Ag nanoparticles will be deposited on the composite surface using a photochemical reduction method, controlling size and density via precursor concentration and irradiation time. The materials will be characterized by XRD, FESEM-EDS, TEM, UV-Vis DRS, PL spectroscopy, and BET. Photocatalytic efficiency will be evaluated by degrading tetracycline hydrochloride under visible light, with active species identified via scavenger experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design principle for integrating plasmonic effects into complex multi-component photocatalytic systems, specifically for antibiotic degradation. It will provide a detailed mechanistic understanding of how plasmonic energy transfer interacts with a Z-scheme charge transfer pathway, contributing to the rational design of high-performance, visible-light-driven nanocatalysts for environmental remediation.
proposals.novelty: While plasmonic enhancement of simple semiconductors is known, its application to tailor and optimize the interfacial charge dynamics in a Z-scheme CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 heterojunction—a system studied for hydrogen storage and dye degradation—for antibiotic removal is novel and non-obvious, creating a multi-modal enhancement strategy.

proposals.title: In-Situ Diagnostic Heterostructures: ALD-Synthesized g-C3N4/CuMn2O4 Nanocatalysts with Engineered Tracer Layers for Real-Time Monitoring of Metal Leaching and Photocatalytic Stability
proposals.motivation: The long-term stability of transition metal oxide photocatalysts in aqueous environments is a critical, yet poorly understood, barrier to their practical application in water treatment. Pre-2026 research, including by Salavati-Niasari's group, focuses on synthesis and activity but often treats degradation and metal leaching as a post-mortem observation. A predictive understanding of how atomic-scale interface engineering influences degradation pathways is lacking, hindering the design of environmentally stable catalysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by using Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD) to create g-C3N4/CuMn2O4 heterostructures with atomically precise interfaces and embedded tracer layers (e.g., isotopically labeled or gradient-doped Mn/Cu), the initial interfacial structure will dictate the kinetics and pathway of metal ion leaching, which can be correlated in real-time to photocatalytic performance decay.
proposals.method: We will synthesize graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets via thermal condensation. A CuMn2O4 spinel layer, with controlled Mn/Cu stoichiometric gradients or doped with a tracer element like stable 65Cu, will be deposited using thermal and plasma-enhanced ALD cycles to ensure atomic-level interface control. The photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue and tetracycline under visible light will be conducted in a flow-cell reactor coupled to an inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS) for continuous effluent analysis. Post-operation characterization will involve in-situ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping to correlate leaching data with interfacial chemical and morphological evolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel methodology for designing 'self-diagnostic' photocatalytic materials that simultaneously degrade pollutants and report their own stability. It will provide the first predictive model linking ALD-engineered interfacial atomic structure to metal leaching kinetics, moving beyond empirical stability assessments. The findings will directly inform the design of safer, more durable nanocatalysts for environmental remediation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the intentional design of the catalyst interface not just for optimal activity, but as an intrinsic sensor for its own degradation, using ALD's precision to encode tracer information. This fusion of advanced synthesis with in-situ diagnostic leaching analysis for predictive stability modeling is a significant departure from conventional post-synthesis stability testing.

proposals.title: Machine Learning-Assisted Inverse Design of g-C3N4-Based S-Scheme Heterojunctions for Synergistic Photocatalytic Remediation of Gasoline Organics and Heavy Metals
proposals.motivation: Industrial wastewater often contains complex mixtures of persistent organic pollutants, like gasoline-derived hydrocarbons, and toxic heavy metal ions, posing a severe environmental threat. Conventional single-pollutant photocatalytic models fail to address this complexity, as they lack tailored redox potentials for simultaneous oxidation and reduction. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a promising visible-light photocatalyst, but its performance in multi-contaminant systems is limited by rapid charge recombination and insufficient redox power.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that machine learning can predict optimal S-scheme semiconductor partners for g-C3N4, creating heterojunctions with precisely engineered band alignments that provide the necessary redox potentials for the concurrent photocatalytic degradation of organic compounds and reduction/immobilization of heavy metal ions.
proposals.method: We will train supervised machine learning models (e.g., Gradient Boosting Regressors and Graph Neural Networks) on pre-2026 materials databases (e.g., Materials Project) to predict bandgap and band edge positions of candidate semiconductors. The models will screen for materials forming S-scheme heterojunctions with g-C3N4, specifically targeting those with conduction band minima more negative than the reduction potentials of target heavy metals (e.g., Cr(VI), Pb(II)) and valence band maxima sufficient to oxidize organics. Top candidates (e.g., bismuth oxyhalides, perovskite oxides) will be synthesized via Salavati-Niasari's established sol-gel and hydrothermal techniques and tested in mixed pollutant systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel ML-guided framework for the rational design of multi-functional S-scheme photocatalysts. It will advance the field from single-pollutant to complex multi-contaminant remediation, providing a validated material system and mechanistic insight into synergistic photocatalytic pathways for co-remediation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of ML for inverse design specifically targeting the electronic structure requirements of a dual redox process (organic oxidation and metal reduction), moving beyond bandgap optimization to address the kinetic and thermodynamic challenges of real-world, mixed wastewater streams.

proposals.title: A Graphene-Enhanced Lu2CrMnO6 Double Perovskite Photoanode for Efficient Photoelectrochemical Water Purification
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants using powder catalysts suffers from poor charge separation and difficult catalyst recovery, limiting practical application. Integrating photocatalytic materials into photoelectrochemical (PEC) systems as immobilized photoanodes offers a promising route to enhance charge extraction and enable continuous operation. This approach remains underexplored for novel double perovskite nanostructures, which show high photocatalytic activity but unoptimized charge transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fabricating a nanocomposite photoanode of Lu2CrMnO6 double perovskite anchored on reduced graphene oxide (rGO) will significantly improve photocurrent density and reduce charge transfer resistance, leading to superior and stable PEC degradation of water-soluble organic dyes compared to its powder form.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Lu2CrMnO6 nanostructures via a sol-gel method, as established in the researcher's prior work, and combine them with rGO via ultrasonication. The composite will be deposited as a thin film on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass using doctor-blading to create the photoanode. The PEC performance will be evaluated under visible-light irradiation using a standard three-electrode cell, measuring photocurrent density, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) for charge transfer resistance, and Mott-Schottky analysis for flat-band potential and carrier density.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel PEC platform based on a double perovskite-graphene composite, directly linking its nanostructure to quantifiable charge separation metrics. It will provide a methodology to transition high-activity powder photocatalysts into efficient, immobilized electrodes, contributing to the design of practical water treatment devices.
proposals.novelty: While the group has synthesized Lu2CrMnO6 for electrochemical hydrogen storage, its application as a photoanode in a PEC cell is entirely novel. The integration of PEC diagnostic techniques to explicitly study charge separation in this double perovskite class, enhanced by graphene, represents a significant advance beyond standard photocatalytic powder testing.

proposals.title: S-Scheme Co2SiO4/g-C3N4/Graphene Heterostructure for Simultaneous Photocatalytic Pollutant Degradation and Hydrogen Evolution
proposals.motivation: Photocatalysis offers a promising route for environmental remediation and clean energy production. However, most systems are designed for a single function, either pollutant degradation or H2 evolution, which is inefficient for practical wastewater treatment where energy recovery is also desirable. Developing a single, stable photocatalyst capable of performing both reactions simultaneously using solar energy is a critical, unmet challenge in sustainable chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing an S-scheme heterojunction by integrating cobalt orthosilicate (Co2SiO4) with graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) and graphene will create a system with strong redox capability, where photogenerated electrons and holes are spatially separated to efficiently reduce protons for H2 evolution and oxidize organic pollutants, respectively, in a single process.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Co2SiO4 nanoparticles via a sol-gel method using amines (e.g., ethylenediamine) as structure-directing agents, based on Prof. Salavati-Niasari's expertise. These will be composited with exfoliated g-C3N4 and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) via ultrasonic-assisted hydrothermal treatment to form an intimate S-scheme interface. The photocatalytic dual-function activity will be evaluated under visible light using a mixture of water-soluble dyes (e.g., methylene blue, rhodamine B) as model pollutants and sacrificial electron donors, with simultaneous monitoring of dye degradation kinetics (UV-Vis) and H2 evolution (gas chromatography).
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel, multifunctional photocatalytic material that concurrently addresses water purification and renewable fuel generation. It will provide fundamental insights into charge carrier dynamics in S-scheme systems under dual-reaction conditions and establish a new design principle for efficient, integrated photocatalytic systems.
proposals.novelty: While S-scheme photocatalysts are known, their application for the simultaneous, co-catalyst-free execution of two distinct redox reactions—complete mineralization of complex organics and proton reduction—in one pot is highly novel and moves beyond typical single-function or sequential process designs.

proposals.title: Enhanced Visible-Light Photocatalytic Degradation of Organic Pollutants via a Novel Heterojunction: In-Situ Growth of Graphitic Carbon Nitride on Pre-Formed Hematite Nanostructures
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, constructing efficient, visible-light-active heterojunction photocatalysts for water purification remains a key challenge. Common synthesis methods for g-C3N4/metal oxide composites often involve mixing pre-formed components, which can lead to poor interfacial contact and limit charge transfer efficiency. A controlled, in-situ growth strategy is needed to create more intimate and effective heterojunctions for superior photocatalytic performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the in-situ thermal polymerization of melamine directly on pre-synthesized, morphology-controlled Fe2O3 nanostructures will create a more intimate and coherent g-C3N4/Fe2O3 interface. This enhanced interfacial contact is expected to significantly improve the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby boosting photocatalytic degradation activity under visible light.
proposals.method: First, α-Fe2O3 nanostructures (e.g., nanorods, porous spheres) will be synthesized via a hydrothermal method using iron precursors and amines, drawing from Salavati-Niasari's expertise in amine-mediated morphology control. These pre-formed Fe2O3 nanostructures will then be uniformly dispersed in a melamine solution. The composite will be subjected to controlled thermal polymerization (e.g., at 550°C under N2) to grow g-C3N4 directly on the Fe2O3 surface. The materials will be characterized using XRD, FESEM/TEM, XPS, UV-Vis DRS, and PL spectroscopy to confirm structure, morphology, chemical state, and optical properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, rational synthesis protocol for creating intimate semiconductor heterojunctions with controlled interfaces. It is expected to yield a highly active, visible-light photocatalyst for degrading organic dyes like methylene blue and rhodamine B, providing mechanistic insight into the role of synthesis order on charge carrier dynamics. The findings will advance the design principles for next-generation composite photocatalysts.
proposals.novelty: While g-C3N4/Fe2O3 composites are known, the deliberate inversion of the synthesis sequence—growing g-C3N4 on pre-formed, tailored Fe2O3—is a non-obvious and rarely explored strategy to engineer the heterojunction interface from the bottom up, rather than simply mixing components.

proposals.title: Systematic Optimization of FeCl3 Precursor Concentration for Enhanced Photocatalytic Degradation Using Fe-based Nanocomposites on Reduced Graphene Oxide
proposals.motivation: Iron-based nanostructures are promising, low-cost photocatalysts for water purification, but their performance is highly sensitive to synthesis parameters. The concentration of the FeCl3 precursor critically influences the morphology, crystallinity, and interfacial properties of the final nanostructure, yet a systematic study defining an optimal concentration threshold for maximizing photocatalytic activity is lacking. Establishing this relationship is crucial for the rational design of efficient, scalable photocatalysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an optimal, narrow concentration window of FeCl3 precursor exists, which yields nanostructures with an ideal balance of high surface area, optimal crystallite size, and strong coupling with a conductive reduced graphene oxide (rGO) support, thereby maximizing charge separation and photocatalytic degradation efficiency.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Fe3O4/α-Fe2O3 nanocomposites anchored on rGO via a solvothermal method, varying only the FeCl3·6H2O concentration (e.g., 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0, 8.0 mM) while keeping other parameters constant. The materials will be characterized using XRD, FESEM, TEM, BET, UV-Vis DRS, and PL spectroscopy. Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated by monitoring the degradation of methylene blue and rhodamine B under visible light, with kinetics analyzed using the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model to determine rate constants.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative guideline for the synthesis of high-performance Fe-based photocatalysts by identifying a precise precursor concentration threshold. It will elucidate the structure-property relationship between precursor concentration, material characteristics, and photocatalytic activity, contributing directly to the field of rational catalyst design for environmental remediation.
proposals.novelty: While many studies vary synthesis parameters, a focused investigation on the isolated effect of FeCl3 concentration threshold, specifically for optimizing photocatalytic degradation on conductive rGO scaffolds, represents a targeted and non-obvious optimization strategy not thoroughly explored in pre-2026 literature on Salavati-Niasari's systems.

proposals.title: Constructing a Z-Scheme Heterojunction of Dicobalt Orthosilicate/Graphene-Modified Graphitic Carbon Nitride for Enhanced Visible-Light Photocatalytic Hydrogen Production
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic water splitting using visible light is a critical pathway for sustainable hydrogen fuel production. However, pre-2026 challenges include the rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers in single-component photocatalysts and the limited visible-light absorption of many efficient materials. Developing stable, visible-light-active heterojunctions is therefore a key research frontier for practical solar energy conversion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a direct Z-scheme heterojunction between p-type dicobalt orthosilicate (Co2SiO4) and n-type graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), interconnected by reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanosheets, will significantly enhance charge separation and visible-light harvesting, leading to superior photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rates from water.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Co2SiO4 nanoparticles via a sol-gel method using amine surfactants (e.g., ethylenediamine) to control morphology, as per Salavati-Niasari's expertise. Bulk g-C3N4 will be prepared by thermal polycondensation of melamine and subsequently exfoliated. The Z-scheme nanocomposite will be assembled through a hydrothermal process, where pre-synthesized Co2SiO4 and exfoliated g-C3N4 are mixed with graphene oxide (GO) and reduced in situ to form Co2SiO4/rGO/g-C3N4. Photocatalytic H2 production will be tested under visible light (λ > 420 nm) using a 300W Xe lamp, with methanol as a sacrificial agent, and the evolved gas quantified by gas chromatography.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, rationally designed Z-scheme photocatalyst system that effectively combines a transition metal silicate with a carbon nitride framework via a graphene bridge. It will provide fundamental insights into the charge transfer mechanism at the solid-solid interface and demonstrate a scalable synthesis route for efficient, visible-light-driven hydrogen generation.
proposals.novelty: While Co2SiO4 and g-C3N4 have been studied separately, their combination into a Z-scheme heterojunction is unexplored. The novelty lies in using rGO not just as a conductive support, but as a tailored electron mediator to facilitate direct Z-scheme charge transfer between these specific semiconductors, optimizing the redox potential for water splitting.

ideas.title: Conductive Carbon-Scaffolded Ternary Hybrid of g-C3N4 and CuMn2O4
ideas.description: This design overcomes the fatal flaw by constructing a robust, three-component architecture. A conductive carbon framework (e.g., rGO) serves as the durable, high-surface-area backbone for structural support and electron highways. Onto this scaffold, g-C3N4 nanosheets and CuMn2O4 nanoparticles are co-immobilized in close proximity, ensuring strong interfacial coupling. This configuration maintains the intended synergistic interaction between the semiconductor and the spinel oxide while guaranteeing mechanical stability, enhanced charge carrier mobility, and resilience under operational conditions for applications like photocatalysis or electrocatalysis.

ideas.title: Mapping Charge Transfer Mechanism as a Function of Composition and Synthesis
ideas.description: This research direction systematically synthesizes CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 composites across a wide matrix of mass ratios and synthesis conditions (e.g., temperature, time, method). For each distinct point in this synthesis parameter space, comprehensive photocatalytic performance data is coupled with advanced characterization (in-situ XPS, ESR, radical trapping, photodeposition) to rigorously test and identify the operative charge transfer mechanism. The core synergy is that optimization is not merely for a single performance metric but for understanding and intentionally steering the fundamental electron-hole pathway. This enables the deliberate design of composites with a targeted charge separation mechanism optimized for a specific photocatalytic reaction.

ideas.title: Adapting Bio-Inspired Scaffold Reversal to Inorganic-Organic Composite Design
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from tissue engineering, where the traditional hierarchy of a stiff inorganic scaffold coated with organic material was successfully inverted to use a soft organic scaffold functionalized with inorganic nanoparticles. The analogous solution involved designing a continuous, porous organic polymer network as the primary structural framework, with inorganic bioactive components deposited onto it to provide surface functionality without bearing the main structural load. Adapted to materials chemistry, this means synthesizing g-C3N4 to form the core continuous support—a 2D conductive, photoresponsive 'soft scaffold'—and then decorating it with CuMn2O4 nanoparticles. The inversion challenges the assumption that the inorganic component must be the structural backbone. Here, g-C3N4 defines the composite's electronic and structural architecture, while CuMn2O4 provides localized, surface-confined catalytic or redox-active sites, potentially leading to novel charge separation pathways and synergistic effects in applications like photocatalysis or sensing, inspired by bio-scaffold design principles.

ideas.title: Geochemically-informed photocatalytic degradation of complex pollutant mixtures
ideas.description: This research direction uses the comprehensive local geochemical baseline for 25 elements to formulate complex, environmentally representative aqueous matrices containing background inorganic ions. The CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 S-scheme photocatalyst is then tested for degrading pharmaceuticals or pesticides within these matrices, moving beyond single-pollutant studies in pure water. This approach reveals how local water chemistry influences photocatalytic efficiency, selectivity, and potential catalyst poisoning or deactivation. The outcome is a practical assessment of the catalyst's real-world applicability and the generation of degradation data that accounts for local geochemical interference.

ideas.title: ML-Guided Inverse S-Scheme Design: g-C3N4-Supported Perovskite Nanodots for Optimized Charge Dynamics
ideas.description: This research integrates machine learning and inverted composite synthesis to systematically design and fabricate novel S-scheme heterojunctions. Machine learning models, trained on existing material databases, are used to predict and screen perovskite and spinel nanoparticles (like CuMn2O4) with optimal band alignments to serve as electron mediators when decorated on a g-C3N4 nanosheet core. The core innovation is the synthesis of these ML-predicted nanoparticles directly onto the g-C3N4 support, deliberately inverting the typical hierarchy to probe charge transfer dynamics in a non-conventional architecture. This approach not only validates ML predictions with experimental data from a novel structural configuration but also uses the results to iteratively refine the models, accelerating the discovery of high-efficiency photocatalytic systems for applications like water splitting.

ideas.title: Spectrum-Dependent Synergistic Degradation of Co-contaminated Petrochemical Wastes
ideas.description: This research will investigate the photocatalytic treatment of soil or water co-contaminated with heavy metals and gasoline-derived organic compounds under controlled spectral conditions (full solar, UV-only, visible-only). The core objective is to determine how the energy input spectrum influences not just the degradation efficiency of the organics, but also the redox speciation, mobility, and potential re-adsorption of the heavy metals during the process. This approach will reveal if certain spectral ranges optimize the simultaneous detoxification of both pollutant classes or if they create antagonistic effects, thereby providing a fundamental basis for designing targeted, solar-spectrum-adaptive remediation strategies for complex petrochemical waste sites.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Deconvolution of Photocatalyst Degradation Pathways Under Extended Cycling
ideas.description: This study moves beyond simple performance cycling to deconstruct the root causes of photocatalyst deactivation. By coupling long-term reusability tests with in-situ spectroscopic and microscopic analysis, we directly correlate drops in activity with specific chemical, structural, and morphological changes. Controlled experiments will isolate stressors like pollutant byproduct adsorption, leaching of dopants, and light-induced structural damage. The outcome is a mechanistic degradation map that informs the rational design of more robust, next-generation photocatalysts, rather than just a durability rating for a single material.

ideas.title: Cycling Redox Stress Tests for Fe-Mn Oxide Photocatalyst Stability and Metal Sequestration
ideas.description: This research will subject a Fe-Mn oxide photocatalyst, designed for contaminant degradation or metal sequestration, to extended cycling tests that combine photocatalytic reuse with simulated soil redox fluctuations. Each cycle will include a photocatalytic reaction phase followed by an induced redox shift (e.g., from oxic to anoxic conditions), after which metal binding stability will be quantified via sequential extraction. This protocol assesses whether the practical reusability of the photocatalyst is compromised by the very environmental redox dynamics it must withstand to perform its secondary function of stable metal immobilization, providing a holistic measure of long-term environmental utility.

ideas.title: ML-Driven Design of g-C3N4 S-Scheme Photocatalysts for Co-Remediation of Heavy Metals and Organic Pollutants
ideas.description: This research integrates machine learning for bandgap and band alignment prediction with the complex chemistry of mixed pollutant systems. Machine learning models will be trained on material databases to predict and screen for optimal S-scheme semiconductor partners for g-C3N4, but the target output is specifically optimized for the co-remediation of gasoline-derived organic compounds and common site-specific heavy metals. The synergy allows for the inverse design of photocatalysts where the predicted electronic structure is tailored to generate the necessary redox potentials to break down persistent organics while also facilitating the concurrent reduction or immobilization of heavy metal ions, moving beyond single-pollutant models to address real-world, multi-contaminant scenarios.

ideas.title: Apply Antenna-Receiver Coupling from Radio Engineering to Plasmonic Enhancement
ideas.description: This idea applies the antenna-receiver coupling principle from Radio Frequency engineering to the problem of enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. In RF systems, a passive resonant antenna captures and concentrates radiation for a receiver. The analogous solution is to design plasmonic nanoparticles (optical antennas) with geometries and compositions tuned to the solar spectrum. These antennas will create intense localized surface plasmon resonances, concentrating light into the CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 composite interface. This adapted approach focuses the plasmonic enhancement on directly amplifying the local electric field within the charge separation region, thereby potentially increasing photocatalytic reaction rates more efficiently than non-resonant light absorption alone.

ideas.title: Scalable Synthesis of Redox-Stable Fe-Mn Oxide Nanocomposites for Dynamic Water Treatment
ideas.description: This research direction develops a scalable manufacturing process for Fe-Mn oxide nanocomposites, but with the synthesis parameters explicitly tuned to maximize the stability of sequestered metals under oscillating redox conditions. The core innovation is that the material's performance benchmark for scale-up is not just adsorption capacity, but also its resilience to reductive dissolution and metal re-mobilization. This enables the production of engineered sorbents suitable for pilot-scale trials in complex, real-world environments like stormwater retention ponds, managed aquifer recharge, or treating mining-influenced waters, where redox conditions are not static. The outcome is a transition from studying a natural soil process to creating a deployable, environmentally robust technology based on that process.

ideas.title: ALD-Engineered Nanocatalysts with In-Situ Leaching Diagnostics for Predictive Environmental Stability
ideas.description: This research direction uses atomic layer deposition (ALD) to fabricate g-C3N4/CuMn2O4 heterostructures with atomically precise interfaces, intentionally designed with tracer elements or layered gradients to act as an intrinsic sensor. During catalytic operation (e.g., in water treatment), advanced spectroscopic and imaging techniques are employed to monitor in real-time the leaching and migration of specific metal species from the engineered interface. This allows for the correlation of initial interfacial atomic structure and bonding, controlled by ALD, with the kinetics and pathways of metal loss, creating a predictive model for catalyst degradation and contaminant spread. The outcome is a dual-function material that performs catalysis while self-reporting its own long-term stability and environmental risk.

ideas.title: City-Scale Screening with Optimized, Site-Specific Nanocomposite Sensors
ideas.description: This research fuses large-scale environmental screening with nanomaterial optimization to develop a next-generation sensor network for Lagos. Instead of using a single, generic sensor, the project will synthesize and characterize a library of CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 nanocomposites with varied ratios and synthesis parameters. These optimized variants will then be deployed strategically across the city's gasoline stations to detect heavy metals, with each sensor's composition potentially tailored to the historical or expected contamination profile of its specific location. This approach enables the creation of a high-fidelity, spatially resolved contamination map while simultaneously determining how nanomaterial performance depends on real-world environmental matrices, closing the loop between lab-based optimization and field application.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Photocatalyst Screening via a Bioassay-Inspired Diagnostic Array
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by applying analogical reasoning from clinical diagnostics. In clinical microbiology, a single sample is tested against an array of diagnostic media in a multi-well plate to identify multiple pathogens simultaneously. Adapted to photocatalysis research, this solution involves designing a high-throughput screening array. A standardized suspension of the CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 S-scheme photocatalyst would be applied to a multi-reactor or multi-well plate system, where each well is pre-loaded with a different target pollutant from a critical set (e.g., ciprofloxacin, atrazine, methylene blue, and bisphenol A) in a consistent aqueous matrix. This approach enables the rapid, parallel evaluation of the catalyst's versatility, degradation kinetics, and potential for forming toxic intermediates across diverse pollutant classes—pharmaceuticals, pesticides, dyes, and endocrine disruptors—mimicking the efficiency of a diagnostic panel to profile catalytic performance broadly.

ideas.title: Isotopic Fingerprinting of Photocatalyst Degradation Pathways Under Controlled Spectral Input
ideas.description: This research fuses isotopic forensics with controlled photocatalytic testing to solve a critical materials science problem: identifying how and why photocatalysts degrade and release metals. By systematically testing identical catalyst samples under full solar spectrum versus UV-only irradiation while simultaneously collecting leachate, the isotopic ratios (e.g., Pb, Ti, Zn) of the released metals are analyzed. The core innovation is that the isotopic 'fingerprint' of the leached metal, when coupled with the specific irradiation condition that produced it, acts as a unique tracer for the dominant degradation pathway, distinguishing between photocorrosion, ligand-driven dissolution, or mechanical wear. This provides a novel method to engineer more stable photocatalytic systems by targeting the degradation mechanism revealed by the isotopic-spectral signature.

ideas.title: Adapting accelerated lifetime testing from battery electrochemistry to photocatalyst durability
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from battery electrochemistry, where accelerated lifetime testing is standard, this research adapts the solution to photocatalyst durability. In battery research, accelerated degradation protocols (using elevated stress factors like temperature and current) combined with intermittent reference performance testing are used to model long-term stability and failure modes efficiently. The adapted idea is to develop a parallel accelerated stress testing protocol for the photocatalyst. This protocol would subject the material to intensified but representative cycling conditions (e.g., higher light flux, reactant concentration, or operational temperature), interspersed with periodic returns to standard baseline conditions for detailed performance benchmarking (activity, selectivity, structural characterization). This methodology aims to decouple different degradation mechanisms (photocorrosion, active site poisoning, structural collapse) and build a predictive model for the catalyst's practical reusability and lifetime over thousands of cycles, providing deeper mechanistic insight than simple extended real-time cycling tests alone.

ideas.title: Mapping S-scheme charge transfer dynamics via time-resolved interfacial Stark spectroscopy
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from biophysics, where voltage-sensitive dyes map electric potential changes in neurons, to the problem of probing S-scheme charge transfer. The analogous solution uses fluorescent reporters whose optical properties change with the local electric field to image signal propagation. Adapted for photocatalysis, the research involves designing or implementing molecular 'Stark reporters' sensitive to interfacial electric fields and integrating them at the heterojunction of an S-scheme photocatalyst. Using advanced in-situ, time-resolved spectroscopic techniques (like ultrafast transient absorption spectroscopy), the spectral shifts of these reporters are monitored. This allows for the direct, real-time observation and mapping of the proposed S-scheme charge carrier dynamics—specifically the generation, direction, and recombination of the transient interfacial electric field—providing unambiguous spectroscopic evidence for the S-scheme mechanism.

ideas.title: Design of Experiments (DoE) for Optimizing Composite Photocatalyst Synthesis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the statistical optimization framework from pharmaceutical formulation science to the materials synthesis problem. The analogous problem—finding the optimal blend of excipients and processing conditions for a drug tablet—is solved using Design of Experiments (DoE) to efficiently map a multi-variable parameter space. The adapted research idea is to apply Response Surface Methodology (RSM) to the CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 composite system. By treating the mass ratio and critical synthesis conditions (e.g., temperature, duration) as design factors in a Central Composite Design, a minimal set of experiments can be performed. The resulting photocatalytic performance data is then used to construct a predictive model, identifying not just the optimal performance point but also the interaction effects between variables and the robustness of the synthesis protocol.

ideas.title: Multi-fidelity ML framework for predicting S-scheme heterojunction formation probability with g-C3N4 via interfacial electronic descriptors
ideas.description: This research designs a machine learning system to predict viable S-scheme photocatalytic partners for g-C3N4 by directly addressing the interfacial electronic structure problem. The core innovation is a pipeline that first uses high-throughput density functional theory (DFT) on a curated set of material interfaces to compute critical interfacial features like band offsets, charge density difference, and work function changes. These interfacial descriptors, combined with standard bulk properties, form the training dataset for a supervised ML model. The final model can then rapidly score new material combinations for their likelihood of forming an effective S-scheme heterojunction, moving beyond simple bulk bandgap alignment to a more physically accurate prediction of interfacial charge transfer behavior.

ideas.title: Solar Spectrum-Driven Photoremediation: Assessing Subsurface Metal Mobilization and Long-Term Sink/Source Dynamics
ideas.description: This research investigates how the spectral quality of solar irradiation (full spectrum vs. UV-only) used in photocatalytic soil remediation influences not just surface contaminant degradation efficiency, but also the vertical mobilization and long-term sequestration of metals in the soil profile. It tests the hypothesis that different light regimes alter photoreaction byproducts and soil chemistry, thereby affecting whether deeper soil layers become a permanent sink or a temporary reservoir for mobilized metal species. The study will correlate photocatalytic energy input with depth-specific metal distribution and speciation over time, challenging the assumption that topsoil analysis alone is sufficient for risk assessment after light-based remediation. This creates a predictive framework for selecting remediation light sources that optimize both surface cleanup and long-term subsurface stability.

ideas.title: Apply Graph Neural Networks for Band Alignment Prediction via Protein-Ligand Docking Analogy
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational drug discovery—using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for predicting protein-ligand binding affinity—to the problem of predicting optimal band alignments for S-scheme photocatalysts. The analogous field is Computational Drug Discovery, where GNNs are trained on databases of known protein-ligand complexes to model the intricate structure-property relationships and enable virtual screening for high-affinity drug candidates. The adapted research idea involves training a GNN model on a materials graph database (e.g., from the Materials Project or OQMD), representing g-C3N4 and candidate partner materials as graphs. The model learns to predict a quantitative metric of effective band alignment (the analog of binding affinity) directly from atomic structure and composition, thereby enabling the rapid, accurate, and structure-aware virtual screening of thousands of potential compounds to identify the most promising S-scheme partners for g-C3N4, significantly accelerating photocatalyst design.

ideas.title: Direct spectroscopic quantification of primary oxidant identity and flux under operando photocatalytic conditions
ideas.description: This redesigned study employs a coupled spectroscopic and chemical trapping approach to unambiguously identify the primary oxidative species in photocatalysis. By using time-resolved in-situ spectroscopy under operando conditions, it directly monitors the formation and decay of surface-trapped holes and adsorbed hydroxyl radicals. Isotopic labeling and a matrix of selective scavengers, applied at varying potentials and pH, allow for the quantification of each species' contribution to overall oxidation activity. This system moves beyond inferential evidence to provide direct, kinetic data on which primary oxidant dominates under specific reaction environments.

ideas.description: This research direction develops a flexible, scalable manufacturing platform for the hybrid nanocomposite that can produce batches tuned to the distinct metal speciation profiles of different contaminated sites. Instead of a single generic material, the process will allow for the adjustable incorporation of specific functional groups or structural properties during synthesis to optimally target the dominant contaminants (e.g., As(III) vs. Cr(VI)) and local geochemical conditions of a given location. This enables the cost-effective production of site-tailored sorbent cartridges or modules for pilot-scale trials, moving beyond one-size-fits-all remediation. The synergy lies in using scalable process engineering to achieve precision remediation, making targeted cleanup strategies practically and economically viable for field deployment.

ideas.title: Hybrid MLD/ALD sequential deposition for perfect conformal coating of g-C3N4 with CuMn2O4
ideas.description: This redesigned approach tackles the critical challenge of achieving a perfect, conformal coating on complex g-C3N4 nanostructures. It employs a two-step, in-situ deposition sequence beginning with Molecular Layer Deposition (MLD) to apply a uniform hybrid organic-inorganic adhesion layer that primes the surface. This layer ensures optimal nucleation sites and surface energy for the subsequent Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD) of the CuMn2O4 catalyst. The result is a guaranteed, ultrathin, and pinhole-free coating that maximizes interfacial contact area and charge transfer efficiency without compromising the porosity or morphology of the underlying g-C3N4 substrate.

ideas.title: Definitive Mechanistic Discrimination via Coupled Time-Resolved and Spatially Resolved In-Situ Spectroscopy
ideas.description: This research employs a coupled spectroscopic approach to unambiguously identify the charge transfer pathway in a photocatalyst, moving beyond fitting static performance data to models. Transient absorption spectroscopy directly measures the lifetimes of photo-induced electrons and holes to confirm interfacial transfer and distinguish direct Z-scheme recombination from S-scheme or Type-II separation. Simultaneously, in-situ irradiated XPS maps the chemical potential and band bending at the junction under operational conditions, providing spatial evidence for the charge flow direction. The correlation of these dynamic and spatial datasets provides a definitive experimental signature to validate one mechanistic model over the others.

ideas.title: In-situ synchrotron X-ray spectroscopy for simultaneous S-scheme dynamics and metal speciation tracking
ideas.description: This research direction combines advanced in-situ spectroscopic probes to directly observe S-scheme charge transfer with synchrotron-based X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) for direct metal speciation. The novel approach uses a single, powerful synchrotron X-ray source to perform time-resolved, in-situ XAS and complementary techniques like resonant inelastic X-ray scattering (RIXS) on a working photocatalytic or electrocatalytic device. This allows researchers to correlate the flow of electrons and holes across the heterojunction interface (the S-scheme dynamics) with simultaneous, element-specific tracking of oxidation state changes and local coordination environments of metal active sites involved in the redox reactions. The synergy provides an unprecedented, atomic-level mechanistic view of how charge separation drives catalytic transformations, moving beyond indirect evidence to direct observation of the coupled electronic and chemical processes.

ideas.title: Design Isotopically-Tagged CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 Composites for Source-Attributed Catalytic Remediation
ideas.description: This research synthesizes CuMn2O4/g-C3N4 composites where the metal precursors (Cu, Mn) are sourced from isotopically distinct, known reservoirs, creating a material with a deliberate isotopic 'fingerprint'. The composite ratio and synthesis parameters are then systematically optimized not only for catalytic performance (e.g., in degrading organic pollutants) but also for preserving and presenting this isotopic signature during use. When deployed at a contaminated site, the composite performs remediation while releasing or altering its isotopic tag, allowing its migration and contribution to be distinguished from the background contamination using forensic isotope analysis. This enables a closed-loop system where the remediation agent itself provides auditable proof of its source and site-specific activity.

ideas.title: Spatio-temporally resolved correlative spectroscopy and photocurrent mapping for direct S-scheme validation
ideas.description: This redesigned approach integrates time-resolved in-situ spectroscopy (like transient absorption or photoluminescence) with a synchronized, micron-scale photocurrent and surface potential mapping system. A custom sample stage features patterned transparent electrodes on the heterojunction, allowing simultaneous spectroscopic probing and direct electrical measurement of charge flow direction and kinetics at the interface under illumination. This correlative data directly links spectroscopic signatures of carrier dynamics to the net vectorial charge separation required by the S-scheme, distinguishing it from parasitic recombination pathways. The system thus provides a causal, rather than correlative, validation of the proposed charge transfer mechanism.

ideas.title: Use multi-model inference from astrophysics to test photocatalytic charge transfer schemes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from astrophysics, where multiple competing models are tested against the same observational data to explain phenomena like dark matter or cosmic inflation. The analogous solution involves using statistical model selection techniques to objectively compare the fit of different theoretical frameworks. Adapted to photocatalysis, we propose to formally test if a Z-scheme or Type-II heterojunction model could explain the observed photocatalytic data by applying a multi-model inference framework. The core photocatalytic dataset (e.g., photocatalytic activity, photoluminescence, time-resolved spectroscopy, band alignment measurements) will be used to fit parameters for the S-scheme, Z-scheme, and Type-II models. Statistical tools like the Bayesian Information Criterion will then be employed to determine which charge transfer mechanism is most strongly supported by the evidence, thereby challenging the assumption that the S-scheme is the only valid interpretation.

ideas.description: This idea applies the solution from industrial catalysis and zeolite design, where materials are engineered with specific structures to selectively capture and stabilize target molecules, to the inverse problem of soil design for metal immobilization. The analogous solution involves designing catalysts with precise pore architectures and active sites for selective adsorption and conversion. Adapted to soil science, the research would engineer composite soil amendments (like tailored biochar-clay hybrids) to function as a 'molecular sieve' for metals. These amendments would be designed with specific surface chemistries, pore sizes, and reactive phases to selectively sequester mobile heavy metal ions and promote their re-mineralization into stable, residual-phase compounds, thereby permanently reducing their bioavailability and mobility.
